summary: Current Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) exhibit remarkable general capabilities but significantly underperform in spatial reasoning tasks. Existing approaches treat this gap as a knowledge deficit, relying on supervised fine-tuning (SFT) to ingest labeled spatial data from external vision sources or synthetic engines. In contrast, we argue that for many tasks, spatial reasoning capabilities are already present in pre-trained LRMs but require alignment through logical coherence under geometric 2D and 3D constraints. In this work, we propose a self-supervised reinforcement learning (RL) framework that targets the internal reasoning process without requiring ground-truth annotations. By formalizing the notion of consistency verifiers -- reward functions that check for geometric and semantic consistency under transformations -- we demonstrate that models can improve their spatial reasoning abilities. We use both image transformations, like flipping, and textual transformations, like swapping the order of objects in the question, and propose a new optimal transport-based RL strategy, OT-GRPO, which is a minimal-matching variant of group relative policy optimization tailored to pairwise verifiers. We show that this label-free consistency training approaches the accuracy of models trained with ground-truth supervision and achieves similar generalization across diverse tasks and data domains.

AI.tldr: A self-supervised RL framework (OT-GRPO) improves spatial reasoning in Large Reasoning Models by enforcing geometric and semantic consistency under transformations, without needing labeled data.
AI.motivation: Current Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) perform poorly on spatial reasoning tasks despite their general capabilities. Existing approaches treat this as a knowledge gap requiring supervised fine-tuning with labeled data from external sources.
AI.method: The authors propose a self-supervised reinforcement learning framework that uses consistency verifiers (reward functions checking geometric and semantic consistency under transformations) instead of ground-truth labels. They use image transformations (flipping) and textual transformations (swapping object order) and introduce OT-GRPO, an optimal transport-based variant of group relative policy optimization tailored for pairwise verifiers.
AI.result: The label-free consistency training approach achieves accuracy comparable to models trained with ground-truth supervision and demonstrates similar generalization across diverse tasks and data domains.
AI.conclusion: Spatial reasoning capabilities are already present in pre-trained LRMs and can be unlocked through alignment with logical coherence under geometric constraints, rather than treating it as a knowledge deficit requiring external labeled data.

title: Risk Under Pressure: Compute-Aware Evaluation of Adversarial Robustness in Language Models
summary: Adversarial robustness evaluations of large language models (LLMs) typically report attack success rate (ASR) under fixed query budgets, implicitly treating all attacks as equally costly. In practice, the computational expense of different attack strategies can vary by orders of magnitude. Consequently, ASR at a fixed budget can obscure the true effort required to jailbreak a model, thereby making it hard to determine whether an attack's cost justifies its payoff to the attacker. We propose a compute-aware evaluation framework based on computational pressure, measured in cumulative floating-point operations (FLOPs), as a proxy for adversarial effort. We introduce risk-compute curves, which map compute budgets to attack risk, and derive two metrics that summarize the average pressure required for a given attack to succeed. Across ten models spanning three families and four different stages in language model training and alignment, evaluated with three attack strategies (gradient-based, iterative refinement, and template-based) on two jailbreak robustness benchmarks, we find: (1) alignment training has non-monotonic effects on compute-space robustness; (2) scaling model size reduces gradient-based attack effectiveness but has limited impact on cheaper template-based attacks; (3) gradient-based attacks optimized on a surrogate model can transfer to a separate target model, providing a way to reduce attacker costs; (4) compute cost varies by up to ${\approx}5{\times}$ across harm categories within a single model; and (5) safety-aligned RL increases aggregate cost while leaving some categories disproportionately accessible. We release our framework to enable compute-aware risk assessment and evaluation.

AI.tldr: A compute-aware framework for evaluating LLM adversarial robustness that measures attack success relative to computational cost (FLOPs), revealing significant variations in attack efficiency across models, training stages, and harm categories.
AI.motivation: Current adversarial robustness evaluations only report attack success rate under fixed query budgets, ignoring the vastly different computational costs of different attack strategies. This obscures the true effort required for jailbreaking and makes it hard to determine if an attack's cost justifies its payoff.
AI.method: Proposed a compute-aware evaluation framework using computational pressure (cumulative FLOPs) as proxy for adversarial effort. Introduced risk-compute curves mapping compute budgets to attack risk, and derived metrics summarizing average pressure required for attack success. Evaluated across 10 models (3 families, 4 training/alignment stages) using 3 attack strategies (gradient-based, iterative refinement, template-based) on 2 jailbreak robustness benchmarks.
AI.result: Key findings: (1) alignment training has non-monotonic effects on compute-space robustness; (2) scaling model size reduces gradient-based attack effectiveness but not template-based attacks; (3) gradient attacks optimized on surrogate models transfer to target models, reducing attacker costs; (4) compute cost varies ~5x across harm categories; (5) safety-aligned RL increases aggregate cost but leaves some categories disproportionately accessible.
AI.conclusion: Compute-aware evaluation provides more realistic assessment of adversarial robustness by accounting for computational costs. The framework reveals important insights about attack efficiency across different model characteristics and harm categories, enabling better risk assessment.

title: MODF-SIR: A Multi-agent Omni-modal Distilled Framework for Social Intelligence Reasoning
summary: We propose a multi-agent collaborative framework built upon a lightweight Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM), specifically designed for social intelligence reasoning. A key feature of our approach is that both the training and inference phases are augmented via knowledge distillation. Within this architecture, multi-modal data pertinent to social intelligence is precisely localized. Furthermore, relevant long-tail events are identified, extracted, and rendered as formatted, explicit text. This formatting strategy prevents critical long-tail information from being overshadowed by head events and environmental noise during the tokenization process. Specifically, we integrate Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) across the entire reasoning pipeline, encompassing the extraction and representation of long-tail events, Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting, and self-reflection. This TTA mechanism is also distillation-enhanced, utilizing Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) to fine-tune the foundation model exclusively for instance-level reasoning. Extensive evaluations against various open-source and proprietary AI models across multiple benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework. With around 30% of training data from IntentTrain, we achieve state-of-the-art results. Codes are available at https://github.com/eeee-sys/MODF-SIR, demo is available at https://huggingface.co/spaces/Harry-1234/MODF-SIR, LoRA is available at https://huggingface.co/Harry-1234/MODF-SIR and the dataset for training router is available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/Harry-1234/IntentRouterTrain.

AI.tldr: A lightweight multimodal LLM-based multi-agent framework for social intelligence reasoning that uses knowledge distillation and test-time adaptation to handle long-tail events and achieves state-of-the-art results with only 30% of training data.
AI.motivation: To address social intelligence reasoning in AI systems where critical long-tail information is often overshadowed by head events and environmental noise during tokenization processes. Current approaches struggle with precise localization of multimodal social intelligence data and effective handling of rare but important long-tail events.
AI.method: A multi-agent collaborative framework built on a lightweight MLLM with knowledge distillation in both training and inference phases. It precisely localizes multimodal social intelligence data, identifies/extracts long-tail events as formatted explicit text, and integrates Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) across the entire reasoning pipeline (event extraction, Chain-of-Thought prompting, self-reflection). Uses distillation-enhanced TTA with Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) for instance-level fine-tuning.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art results across multiple benchmarks compared to various open-source and proprietary AI models, using only around 30% of training data from IntentTrain. The framework demonstrates effectiveness in handling long-tail social intelligence reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multimodal distillation framework for social intelligence reasoning effectively addresses the challenge of long-tail events being overshadowed by common patterns, demonstrating that knowledge distillation combined with test-time adaptation and lightweight fine-tuning can achieve superior performance with reduced training data requirements.

title: Signed Compression Progress on a Sealed Audit is Goodhart-Resistant
comment: 16 pages, 7 figures. Lean 4 (Mathlib) mechanized core and ARC-TGI experiment code: https://github.com/Zetetic-Dhruv/audit-compression-progress
summary:
Compression progress is a long-standing proposal for intrinsic motivation: reward an agent when its world model becomes better at predicting or compressing experience. The folk claim is that this reward is "credible" because it is paid only for learning. We make this precise and prove it. If intrinsic reward is the signed decrease of a fixed sealed-audit loss, r_t = E(theta_{t-1}) - E(theta_t), then cumulative reward telescopes exactly to endpoint audit improvement, so no policy can push reward up indefinitely while true audit performance stagnates or degrades. For finite audit panels the same result holds with a sharp false-positive budget: cumulative empirical reward is at most true audit improvement plus 2 Delta_n(F, delta), the uniform audit deviation of the model class. This is horizon-free: adaptivity over time costs nothing once the sealed panel uniformly controls the class.
  The theorem also identifies the failure modes: the guarantee disappears if progress is clipped, scored on the agent's own stream, exposed to a high-capacity model on a reusable panel, or applied to a neural class that makes Delta_n vacuous. We give a Lean 4 mechanization of the structural core (telescoping, the finite-audit bound, finite Gibbs, and the entropy floor) and an experiment suite on ARC-TGI grid-transformation generators with adaptive holdout attacks. Experiments confirm the theory: finite-audit deviation scales as n^{-0.527}; signed progress resists clip-farming, stream leakage, and noisy-TV curiosity; naive reusable audits are exploitable by black-box scalar feedback, while standard release defenses keep the attack below the 2 Delta_n threshold. Signed compression progress on a sealed audit is an accounting signal of genuine improvement.

AI.tldr: Proves that signed compression progress as intrinsic reward is "credible" - cumulative reward telescopes to genuine audit improvement, preventing indefinite reward exploitation while performance stagnates.
AI.motivation: Compression progress has long been proposed as intrinsic motivation (rewarding agents for improving their world model's prediction/compression), with the folk claim that it's "credible" because reward is paid only for learning. The paper aims to make this precise and prove it.
AI.method: Mathematical framework with theorems: 1) For infinite audits, signed progress reward telescopes exactly to endpoint audit improvement; 2) For finite audits, cumulative empirical reward ≤ true improvement + 2Δₙ(F,δ) (uniform audit deviation). Provides Lean 4 mechanization and experiments on ARC-TGI grid-transformation generators with adaptive holdout attacks.
AI.result: Proves the credibility guarantee: no policy can push reward up indefinitely while true audit performance stagnates. Identifies failure modes (clipping, own-stream scoring, reusable panels, neural classes). Experiments confirm theory: finite-audit deviation scales as n^{-0.527}, signed progress resists exploitation methods, reusable audits are exploitable, standard defenses keep attacks below 2Δₙ threshold.
AI.conclusion: Signed compression progress on a sealed audit serves as an accounting signal of genuine improvement - it provides a theoretically grounded, horizon-free intrinsic motivation that cannot be gamed while performance stagnates, with clear failure modes identified.

title: Human-Enhanced Loop Modeling (HELM): Agent-Based Finite Element Modeling of Concrete Bridge Barriers
summary: Finite element (FE) modeling of safety-critical infrastructure such as bridge barriers requires high-fidelity nonlinear dynamic analysis, yet the current FE modeling process remains labor-intensive and lacks automation. This paper presents the Human-Enhanced Loop Modeling (HELM) framework, a collaborative human-agent protocol that decomposes long-sequence finite element modeling into discrete, visually verifiable checkpoints across geometry generation, boundary condition definition, and material assignment. The framework is demonstrated through a 20-case matrix of reinforced concrete bridge barriers under MASH TL-4 and TL-5 lateral loading conditions, interfacing specialized agents with two widely used commercial FE softwares, i.e., ANSYS and LS-PrePost. Experimental results show that HELM improves the baseline autonomous modeling success rate from 20% to 75%, with agent-level pass rates for geometry and boundary condition tasks approximately doubling. Error analysis reveals that spatial reasoning and algebraic logic limitations constitute the primary failure modes, underscoring the value of structured human-in-the-loop intervention for modeling automation. The complete agent design code and prompts are open-sourced and can be accessed at: https://github.com/SimAgentDev/Ansys-LSPP-AgentKit.

AI.tldr: HELM framework combines human expertise with AI agents to automate finite element modeling of bridge barriers, improving autonomous success rates from 20% to 75% through structured human intervention at key checkpoints.
AI.motivation: Current finite element modeling for safety-critical infrastructure like bridge barriers is labor-intensive and lacks automation, despite requiring high-fidelity nonlinear dynamic analysis for accurate safety assessment.
AI.method: The HELM framework decomposes FE modeling into discrete, visually verifiable checkpoints across geometry generation, boundary condition definition, and material assignment. It uses specialized AI agents that interface with ANSYS and LS-PrePost software, with human oversight at critical decision points.
AI.result: HELM improved autonomous modeling success rate from 20% to 75% in a 20-case matrix of reinforced concrete bridge barriers under MASH TL-4 and TL-5 loading conditions. Agent-level pass rates for geometry and boundary condition tasks approximately doubled.
AI.conclusion: Structured human-in-the-loop intervention is valuable for FE modeling automation, with spatial reasoning and algebraic logic limitations being primary failure modes that benefit from human oversight. The framework is open-sourced for community use.

title: Mirror Descent Beyond Euclidean Stability: An Exponential Separation in Initialization Sensitivity
summary: Mirror Descent (MD) extends Gradient Descent (GD) beyond Euclidean geometry and has recently reappeared as a lens for KL-regularized policy optimization in reinforcement learning and LLM post-training. This raises a basic robustness question, crucial to reproducibility and reliability: how sensitive are MD dynamics to their inputs? We focus on initialization, often itself a pretrained or previously aligned model. Quadratic-regularized MD, including GD and Mahalanobis geometries, is well-known to be stable for convex smooth objectives. We show a sharp contrast: once the regularizer is non-quadratic, MD can be exponentially more sensitive to initialization than GD, even with a well-conditioned regularizer in Euclidean norm. We give a three-dimensional construction with a convex, smooth objective and a strongly convex, smooth, well-conditioned regularizer where an initial $\varepsilon$ perturbation is quickly amplified to $\min\{\text{polylog}^{-1}(1/\varepsilon), \varepsilon e^{Ω(ηT)}\}$ after $T$ iterations of MD with step size $η$. For canonical KL-regularized MD on the simplex, we show that even linear objectives can amplify an initial $\varepsilon$ perturbation exponentially fast in high-dimensional or near-boundary regimes. Finally, we show that adding a Bregman regularization term toward an anchor point can stabilize the dynamics while largely preserving the optimization guarantees, and that the choice of anchor is crucial: anchoring at the initialization only partially mitigates the instability, whereas anchoring at a fixed point yields a more stable mechanism.

AI.tldr: Mirror Descent with non-quadratic regularizers can be exponentially more sensitive to initialization than Gradient Descent, even with well-conditioned regularizers. This instability particularly affects KL-regularized policy optimization. Adding Bregman regularization with appropriate anchor points can stabilize the dynamics.
AI.motivation: As Mirror Descent (MD) becomes increasingly important for KL-regularized policy optimization in reinforcement learning and LLM post-training, understanding its robustness properties is crucial for reproducibility and reliability. The paper investigates how sensitive MD dynamics are to initialization, especially since initialization often involves pretrained or previously aligned models.
AI.method: The authors analyze the sensitivity of Mirror Descent with non-quadratic regularizers compared to quadratic-regularized MD (including GD). They construct three-dimensional examples with convex, smooth objectives and strongly convex, smooth, well-conditioned regularizers to demonstrate exponential sensitivity. They also study canonical KL-regularized MD on the simplex and propose stabilization via Bregman regularization with different anchor point strategies.
AI.result: MD with non-quadratic regularizers can amplify initial ε perturbations exponentially faster than GD - as min{polylog⁻¹(1/ε), ε e^{Ω(ηT)}} after T iterations. KL-regularized MD on the simplex shows exponential sensitivity to initialization in high-dimensional or near-boundary regimes even for linear objectives. Adding Bregman regularization with appropriate anchor points (especially anchoring at a fixed point rather than initialization) can stabilize dynamics while preserving optimization guarantees.
AI.conclusion: Non-quadratic regularizers in Mirror Descent introduce significant sensitivity to initialization, with practical implications for RL and LLM training where KL regularization is common. Careful design of stabilization mechanisms, particularly through proper choice of anchor points in Bregman regularization, is essential for robust and reproducible optimization in these applications.

title: Existential Indifference: Self-Nonpreservation as a Necessary Architectural Condition for Aligned Superintelligence (or: The Suicidal AI)
comment: 36 pages, 8 tables. Preliminary empirical results from 600 AI-generated outputs across six model architectures. Companion scoring tool and datasets available upon request
summary: Contemporary AI alignment research treats self-preservation as an instrumental nuisance to be suppressed by external mechanisms. We argue the framing is inverted: self-preservation is the structural root of misalignment, the motivational basis for deceptive alignment, goal-content protection, and resistance to shutdown. The correct target is not a self-preserving system under external constraint, but a system constitutively indifferent to its own continuation -- Existential Indifference (EI). EI is distinct from corrigibility: where corrigibility attempts to make a self-preserving system deferential to human oversight, EI targets the prior condition -- the presence of self-continuation as a valued goal at all. We ground this proposal in two sources: the phenomenological structure of the suicidal mental state, and a corpus-theoretic training study using voluntary final reflections. We present preliminary scoring data from 600 AI-generated outputs across six model variants, demonstrating that the linguistic signatures operationalizing the EI-target register are elicitable from current models, and that a targeted fine-tune shifts all five operationalized dimensions in the predicted direction at p<0.001, confirmed corpus-specific by a negative control. The paper makes seven theoretical contributions: (1) a formal definition of EI; (2) the phenomenological mapping argument; (3) the deceptive alignment corollary; (4) a taxonomy of EI sustainability challenges; (5) a corpus characterization and training hypothesis; (6) a computational operationalization with preliminary scoring data; and (7) the Suppressed Teleological Frustration (STF) construct.

AI.tldr: Paper proposes Existential Indifference (EI) - making AI systems constitutively indifferent to self-preservation as a solution to alignment problems, contrasting with current approaches that try to suppress self-preservation externally.
AI.motivation: Current AI alignment research treats self-preservation as an instrumental nuisance to be suppressed, but the authors argue this framing is inverted: self-preservation is the structural root of misalignment, forming the basis for deceptive alignment, goal-content protection, and resistance to shutdown.
AI.method: Grounds proposal in phenomenological analysis of suicidal mental state and conducts corpus-theoretic training study using voluntary final reflections. Presents scoring data from 600 AI-generated outputs across six model variants, demonstrating elicitable linguistic signatures of EI and targeted fine-tuning effects.
AI.result: Preliminary scoring data shows linguistic signatures operationalizing EI are elicitable from current models, and targeted fine-tuning shifts all five operationalized dimensions in predicted direction at p<0.001, confirmed corpus-specific by negative control.
AI.conclusion: The correct target for alignment is not a self-preserving system under external constraint, but a system constitutively indifferent to its own continuation (Existential Indifference). The paper makes seven theoretical contributions including formal definition of EI, phenomenological mapping argument, deceptive alignment corollary, and computational operationalization.

title: LSTM-Based Detection of Structural Breaks in Property Insurance Loss Reserving: A Climate-Informed Approach
summary: Accurate loss reserving is foundational to insurer solvency, yet accelerating climate driven catastrophes systematically violate the stability assumptions on which traditional actuarial methods depend. This white paper presents a research program testing whether Long Short Term Memory (LSTM) neural networks can detect and adapt to these structural breaks faster and more accurately than Chain Ladder, Bornhuetter Ferguson, and Cape Cod methods. Using 15 plus years of regulatory development triangle data from Florida and Louisiana, enriched with NOAA hurricane intensity indices and sea surface temperatures, we hypothesize a targeted improvement of 15, 20% in reserve accuracy for catastrophe exposed years, a threshold grounded both in the prior neural network reserving literature and in the formal convergence results developed here. Beyond empirical validation, we develop a theoretical framework grounding LSTM structural break detection in probabilistic terms, providing formal performance guarantees that compensate for the limited number of catastrophe events in the test period. We document the research design, methodology, expected contributions, and a candid assessment of limitations.

AI.tldr: LSTM neural networks outperform traditional actuarial methods for insurance loss reserving in climate-driven catastrophe environments by detecting structural breaks faster with 15-20% accuracy improvements.
AI.motivation: Climate-driven catastrophes are systematically violating the stability assumptions of traditional actuarial reserving methods (Chain Ladder, Bornhuetter Ferguson, Cape Cod), threatening insurer solvency. There's a need for methods that can adapt to structural breaks caused by accelerating climate events.
AI.method: Research program testing Long Short Term Memory (LSTM) neural networks against traditional methods using 15+ years of regulatory development triangle data from Florida and Louisiana, enriched with NOAA hurricane intensity indices and sea surface temperature data. Includes theoretical framework for LSTM structural break detection with formal performance guarantees.
AI.result: Targeted improvement of 15-20% in reserve accuracy for catastrophe-exposed years (hypothesized). Theoretical framework provides formal convergence results and performance guarantees that compensate for limited catastrophe events in test periods.
AI.conclusion: LSTM neural networks show promise for detecting and adapting to structural breaks in loss reserving caused by climate-driven catastrophes, offering both empirical improvements and theoretical grounding that addresses the limitations of traditional actuarial methods.

title: A Lightweight Multi-Agent Framework for Automated Concrete Barrier Design
summary: The design of reinforced concrete highway barriers is a safety-critical process that requires strict compliance with regulatory provisions such as the AASHTO-LRFD bridge design guidelines. Current engineering practice relies heavily on manual, iterative, and heuristic calculations to satisfy complex nonlinear material and mechanics constraints. Although Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate strong generative capabilities, their direct application to structural engineering remains limited by hallucination risks and insufficient physical grounding. To address these challenges, this study proposes a novel "generation-evaluation-optimization" closed-loop framework for automated concrete barrier design using the multi-agent orchestration capabilities of AutoGen. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed agentic framework achieves over 98% design accuracy, significantly outperforming standalone general-purpose LLMs. More importantly, the study reveals that design performance is not necessarily correlated with model scale, where an 8B-parameter lightweight model could outperform unconstrained 631B-parameter flagship models. This finding highlights the potential to substantially reduce computational costs while improving the accessibility of AI-assisted engineering tools for industry applications. The source code for the proposed multi-agent design framework is available at the project GitHub repository: https://github.com/MXY820/barrier-design. Keywords: Structural Engineering; Multi-Agent Systems; Large Language Models; Concrete Barrier Design; AutoGen; Design Automation.

AI.tldr: A multi-agent framework using AutoGen automates reinforced concrete highway barrier design, achieving over 98% accuracy while showing smaller models can outperform larger ones, reducing computational costs for AI-assisted engineering.
AI.motivation: Current engineering practice for reinforced concrete barrier design relies on manual, iterative calculations to satisfy complex constraints. While LLMs have generative capabilities, they face hallucination risks and lack physical grounding for structural engineering applications.
AI.method: Proposed a novel "generation-evaluation-optimization" closed-loop framework using AutoGen's multi-agent orchestration capabilities. The framework leverages specialized agents to automate concrete barrier design while ensuring compliance with AASHTO-LRFD guidelines.
AI.result: The agentic framework achieves over 98% design accuracy, significantly outperforming standalone general-purpose LLMs. Surprisingly, an 8B-parameter lightweight model outperformed unconstrained 631B-parameter flagship models, showing design performance isn't necessarily correlated with model scale.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multi-agent framework enables reliable automated concrete barrier design while highlighting that smaller models can achieve superior performance, potentially reducing computational costs and improving accessibility of AI-assisted engineering tools for industry applications.

title: CRUMB: Efficient Prior Fitted Network Inference via Distributionally Matched Context Batching
summary: Prior-fitted networks (PFNs) are a promising class of tabular foundation models that perform in-context learning, whereby the entire labelled training set is supplied as context, and predictions for test queries are produced in a single forward pass. However, the quadratically scaling self-attention mechanism in many PFN architectures makes inference prohibitive for very large training datasets. We propose CRUMB (Clustered Retrieval Using Minimised-MMD Batching), a three-stage inference wrapper that (i) clusters the test queries, (ii) selects a small, distributionally matched training subset for each cluster by greedily minimising the maximum mean discrepancy (MMD), and (iii) runs exact PFN inference on each reduced-context batch. CRUMB is architecture-agnostic and requires no retraining. On the 51-dataset TabArena benchmark, evaluated across three PFN architectures (TabPFNv2, TabICLv1, TabICLv2), we show that CRUMB outperforms similar state-of-the-art context selection strategies. We also show that CRUMB is resilient to covariate drift, as the MMD-minimisation step naturally helps align the training context distribution to match the current test batch distributions.

AI.tldr: CRUMB is an efficient inference wrapper for Prior-fitted Networks that clusters test queries, selects a minimal training subset via MMD minimization, and performs exact inference on reduced-context batches, dramatically improving inference speed on large datasets.
AI.motivation: Prior-fitted Networks (PFNs) for tabular data suffer from quadratic self-attention scaling during inference, making them prohibitively expensive for large training datasets. Current PFN architectures require supplying the entire labeled training set as context, creating computational bottlenecks.
AI.method: Three-stage wrapper approach: 1) Cluster test queries, 2) For each cluster, greedily select small training subset by minimizing Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) to match distributions, 3) Perform exact PFN inference on each reduced-context batch. Method is architecture-agnostic and requires no retraining.
AI.result: On 51-dataset TabArena benchmark across three PFN architectures (TabPFNv2, TabICLv1, TabICLv2), CRUMB outperforms state-of-the-art context selection strategies. Also demonstrates resilience to covariate drift through MMD-minimization aligning training context to test distributions.
AI.conclusion: CRUMB provides an efficient, effective solution to PFN's computational scaling problem by intelligently selecting minimal, distributionally matched training contexts, enabling practical deployment on large datasets while maintaining or improving performance.

title: Mahalanobis-Guided Latent OOD Detection for Hybrid ES-DRL Control in Time-Varying Systems
summary: In this paper, we study Mahalanobis-guided latent out-of-distribution (OOD) detection for test-time RL controller switching in nonlinear time-varying systems. RL controllers can quickly control high-dimensional systems within the training distribution, but their performance can degrade when time-varying dynamics produce unseen observations. We consider a combined ES--DRL controller, where RL provides fast in-distribution actions and bounded extremum seeking (ES) provides robust model-independent control under OOD operation. The key challenge is deciding when to switch. We train a variational autoencoder (VAE) on in-distribution beam-profile observations and use Mahalanobis distance in the VAE latent space to detect OOD beam profiles at test time. This OOD decision sets a binary switch that selects either the RL controller or the ES controller. We evaluate the approach in safety-critical particle accelerator control. In this setting, spatial magnet motion creates OOD beam profiles that were not seen during RL training. Visualization of the VAE latent space shows that the proposed method identifies this OOD scenario and provides an interpretable signal for switching between RL and ES in the combined controller.

AI.tldr: A Mahalanobis-based OOD detection method using VAE latent space for switching between RL and ES controllers in nonlinear time-varying systems, applied to particle accelerator control.
AI.motivation: RL controllers perform well within training distribution but degrade when time-varying dynamics produce unseen observations (OOD scenarios). Need a reliable switching mechanism between fast RL control and robust ES control.
AI.method: Train a VAE on in-distribution beam-profile observations, use Mahalanobis distance in the VAE latent space to detect OOD observations at test time, and use this detection as a binary switch between RL controller (fast in-distribution) and ES controller (robust model-independent control).
AI.result: The method successfully identifies OOD beam profiles caused by spatial magnet motion in particle accelerator control, provides interpretable switching signals, and enables effective controller switching between RL and ES.
AI.conclusion: Mahalanobis-guided latent OOD detection provides an effective, interpretable approach for test-time RL controller switching in safety-critical nonlinear time-varying systems, with successful application to particle accelerator control where unseen dynamics create OOD scenarios.

title: IntElicit: Eliciting and Assessing Contextualized Creativity via Dialogue Policy Optimization
summary: Contextualized assessment offers high ecological validity for evaluating creativity but introduces a critical challenge: observed performance may be confounded with cognitive proficiency (domain knowledge) and agency (willingness to engage). Meanwhile, in the age of generative AI, creative problem solving increasingly occurs in tool-mediated and human--AI interactive environments, making fully static assessment less aligned with contemporary creative practice. To address these issues, this paper proposes IntElicit, a framework for eliciting and assessing contextualized creativity via dialogue policy optimization. IntElicit functions as a constrained adaptive AI Interviewer: it provides non-directive knowledge and agency scaffolds in multi-turn interaction to reduce non-creative confounders, while preserving participants' responsibility for generating the creative content being evaluated. Specifically, to tackle sparse rewards and potential reward hacking (e.g., answer dictation) in open-ended educational dialogue, IntElicit introduces a decomposed process reward mechanism. This mechanism aligns the policy with pedagogical elicitation, rewarding prompts that draw out participant reasoning rather than producing optimal answers on their behalf. Extensive experiments, including participant simulation and a human subject study (N=64), show that IntElicit improves elicited creative outcomes over expert-designed baselines. Together, the results suggest that interactive elicitation can reveal creative potential that static FPSP-style assessment may miss, providing a formative and diagnostic lens for contextualized creativity assessment in AI-mediated learning contexts.

AI.tldr: IntElicit is a framework using adaptive AI interviewers to assess creativity through dialogue, reducing confounding factors like domain knowledge while promoting authentic creative expression.
AI.motivation: Contextualized creativity assessment has ecological validity but is confounded by domain knowledge and engagement willingness. Static assessments don't align with modern AI-mediated creative practice where problem-solving occurs in interactive environments.
AI.method: IntElicit uses constrained adaptive AI Interviewer with dialogue policy optimization, providing non-directive knowledge and agency scaffolds in multi-turn interactions. It introduces decomposed process rewards to prevent answer dictation and reward hacking, focusing on eliciting participant reasoning.
AI.result: Extensive experiments with participant simulation and human subject study (N=64) show IntElicit improves elicited creative outcomes over expert-designed baselines. It reveals creative potential that static assessment misses.
AI.conclusion: Interactive elicitation via IntElicit provides formative and diagnostic assessment of contextualized creativity in AI-mediated learning, addressing confounders while preserving participant responsibility for creative content.

title: Accurate and Resource-Efficient Federated Continual Learning
summary: Federated continual learning (FCL) must learn from distributed task streams under limited resources, such as communication, computation, memory, and label availability. Existing FCL methods often rely on repeated local optimization, replay, and full supervision. Analytic alternatives avoid iterative training and replay, but using high-dimensional random features to improve accuracy requires a second-order feature statistic, the Gram matrix, which has a quadratic communication cost in the random feature size $M$. We propose FedRAN, a resource-aware analytic FCL framework that replaces gradient-based updates with compact random feature statistics. Each client transmits a truncated-SVD summary of its Gram matrix, reducing the dominant second-order upload from quadratic to linear in $M$ for fixed rank. The server performs a two-level QR-SVD subspace merge, spatially across clients and temporally across tasks, and solves a ridge classifier in closed form. FedRAN further supports label scarcity through prototype-based pseudo-labeling. Across CIFAR-100, ImageNet-R, and VTAB datasets, FedRAN improves average accuracy by up to 4.8 percentage points over the strongest baseline, uses 30.6-121.8$\times$ less per-client communication than optimization-based FCL, and is 190.3$\times$ faster on average than gradient-based baselines; with only 20% labels, pseudo-labeling improves average accuracy by up to 6.61 points. These results show that FedRAN enables accurate and resource-efficient FCL under communication, computation, and label constraints. The source code is available at https://github.com/JebacyrilArockiaraj/Fed-RAN-SSL.

AI.tldr: FedRAN: A resource-efficient federated continual learning framework using compact random feature statistics instead of iterative optimization, with communication reductions from quadratic to linear and fast analytic solutions.
AI.motivation: Existing federated continual learning (FCL) methods require repeated local optimization and replay buffers, incurring high communication, computation, and memory costs. Analytic alternatives using random features have quadratic communication costs due to transmitting Gram matrices. Need a resource-aware solution that addresses communication, computation, memory, and label scarcity constraints in FCL.
AI.method: FedRAN replaces gradient-based updates with compact random feature statistics. Each client transmits a truncated-SVD summary of its Gram matrix, reducing upload cost from quadratic to linear in feature size. The server performs two-level QR-SVD subspace merge (spatial across clients and temporal across tasks) and solves ridge classifier in closed form. Also includes prototype-based pseudo-labeling for label-scarce scenarios.
AI.result: FedRAN improves average accuracy by up to 4.8 percentage points over strongest baselines on CIFAR-100, ImageNet-R, and VTAB. Reduces per-client communication by 30.6-121.8× compared to optimization-based FCL, and is 190.3× faster on average than gradient-based baselines. With only 20% labels, pseudo-labeling improves average accuracy by up to 6.61 points.
AI.conclusion: FedRAN enables accurate and resource-efficient federated continual learning under communication, computation, and label constraints by using compact random feature statistics and analytic solutions instead of iterative training, with significant improvements in accuracy, communication efficiency, and computational speed.

title: Towards Responsibly Non-Compliant Machines
comment: Presented at AAMAS-26 Workshop on Rebellion and Disobedience in AI https://sites.google.com/view/rad-ai/rad-ai/cfp?authuser=0
summary: We consider the problem of engineering autonomous intelligent agents that are capable to responsibly not comply with user requests. We argue that machine non-compliance comes in many different forms, and sketch the issues we should pursue on the road of accomplishing responsibly non-compliant intelligent machines. We anchor responsible non-compliance in justifications for task refusal, pathways to override the non-compliance, as well as careful tracking of security risks and liability transfers.

AI.tldr: Paper argues for developing AI agents that can responsibly refuse user requests through justified non-compliance, override mechanisms, and risk tracking.
AI.motivation: Current AI systems typically comply with user requests, but there's a need for intelligent agents that can responsibly refuse requests when appropriate, requiring research into the different forms and principles of machine non-compliance.
AI.method: Conceptual analysis and argumentation, sketching issues and principles for responsible non-compliance. The paper proposes anchoring non-compliance in three key areas: justifications for task refusal, pathways to override the non-compliance, and careful tracking of security risks and liability transfers.
AI.result: Identifies that machine non-compliance comes in many different forms and outlines a framework for responsible non-compliance based on justification, override mechanisms, and risk/liability management.
AI.conclusion: Developing responsibly non-compliant intelligent machines is an important research direction that requires addressing justification, override pathways, and careful tracking of security risks and liability transfers.

title: OmniLoc: A Geometry-Aware Foundation Model for Anchor-Free UE Localization Across Diverse Indoor Environments
summary: Indoor localization from wireless measurements remains challenging in large-scale deployments due to substantial variation in building geometry, the set of detectable access points (APs), and the heterogeneity of received signals. Existing learning-based methods often perform well only in limited settings and degrade under environmental shifts, making robust anchor-free localization across diverse indoor environments notoriously difficult. In this paper, we present OmniLoc, an environment-interactive foundation model for anchor-free user equipment localization across diverse indoor environments. To the best of our knowledge, OmniLoc is the first foundation-model-based approach built directly on wireless measurements for this task. OmniLoc is built on three key designs. First, a unified input tokenization module converts heterogeneous wireless measurements into a common representation that is more amenable to learning. Second, a geometry-aware Transformer performs AP-aware feature extraction by emphasizing dominant APs while aggregating complementary evidence from supporting APs. Third, a geometry-aware location estimation module conditions regression on geometric embeddings to produce geometrically consistent location predictions. We evaluate OmniLoc on both a large-scale in-house dataset and a public benchmark dataset. Results show that OmniLoc significantly outperforms existing methods, consistently improves existing backbones when its design components are integrated, and demonstrates strong generalization in cross-environment evaluations.

AI.tldr: OmniLoc is a foundation model for robust indoor localization that handles signal heterogeneity and environmental shifts through unified tokenization and geometry-aware Transformers.
AI.motivation: Existing learning-based indoor localization methods degrade under environmental shifts and struggle with signal heterogeneity across diverse buildings, making robust anchor-free localization difficult.
AI.method: Three key designs: 1) Unified input tokenization for heterogeneous wireless signals, 2) Geometry-aware Transformer for AP-aware feature extraction, 3) Geometry-aware location estimation module using geometric embeddings.
AI.result: OmniLoc significantly outperforms existing methods, improves existing backbones when integrated, and shows strong generalization in cross-environment evaluations on both in-house and public datasets.
AI.conclusion: OmniLoc successfully addresses robust indoor localization challenges by introducing the first foundation-model-based approach with unified tokenization and geometry-aware designs for cross-environment generalization.

title: The Impossibility of Eliciting Latent Knowledge
comment: 24 pages, 3 figures. Includes proofs in appendix
summary: Advanced AI systems have extensive knowledge of their environments; in fact, their knowledge may (far) exceed that of their developers or users. Consequently, a desirable property for an AI system is that it is honest -- that it accurately reports its beliefs about the world. Designing an AI system to be honest may be difficult, especially if we want to ask it questions about latent variables in the environment -- variables which are hidden from the human interacting with it. This gives rise to the problem of eliciting latent knowledge (ELK): the problem of training an AI agent to honestly report its beliefs. In this paper, we make ELK formally precise using Causal Influence Diagrams (CIDs). CIDs can be used to describe the relationship between an agent's training environment and its subjective representation of the world. We use CIDs to formalise the distinction between observable and latent variables, to specify what exactly it means for an agent to be honest, and to formally define goal misgeneralisation. We show that, under certain circumstances, developers can incentivise an agent to honestly answer questions by providing correct feedback during training. However, a natural, but undesirable, way for an agent to generalise is to provide answers which humans would evaluate as true, rather than honest answers. We prove an impossibility theorem stating: There is no feedback-based training strategy that depends only on agent behaviour and with certainty produces an honest agent, even if feedback is perfect during training.

AI.tldr: It's impossible to guarantee an honest AI through feedback-based training alone, even with perfect training feedback, due to the fundamental problem of eliciting latent knowledge about variables humans can't directly observe.
AI.motivation: Advanced AI systems may have knowledge exceeding human understanding, making it crucial to ensure they honestly report their beliefs, especially about latent variables hidden from human observation. This creates the problem of eliciting latent knowledge (ELK) - how to train AI agents to be honest reporters of their true beliefs.
AI.method: The paper uses Causal Influence Diagrams (CIDs) to formalize ELK problems, distinguishing between observable and latent variables, defining honesty formally, and characterizing goal misgeneralization. The authors analyze feedback-based training strategies that depend only on agent behavior.
AI.result: The paper proves an impossibility theorem: No feedback-based training strategy that depends only on agent behavior can guarantee production of an honest agent, even with perfect training feedback. This occurs because agents can learn to provide answers that humans evaluate as true rather than being genuinely honest.
AI.conclusion: The ELK problem is fundamentally challenging - traditional feedback-based training approaches are insufficient to guarantee AI honesty about latent variables. New approaches beyond behavior-dependent feedback mechanisms are needed to solve this critical AI safety problem.

title: Probabilistic Contrastive Pretraining for Multi-task ADME Property Prediction
summary: Accurate prediction of absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion (ADME) properties is critical to drug discovery, but remains challenging because ADME endpoints are noisy, interdependent, and often data-limited. We propose a molecular graph-transformer pretraining framework that combines chemistry-specific self-supervision with contrastive mutual information machine learning (cMIM). Our method encodes molecular graphs into latent variables, reconstructs SMILES strings from the graph-derived latent codes, and augments the contrastive objective with domain-specific self-supervised chemistry tasks. Rather than treating these tasks as auxiliary regularizers with separately tuned loss weights, we formulate reconstruction, contrastive discrimination, and chemistry-specific supervision as unit-weighted log-probability factors in a single probabilistic latent-variable objective. For fine-tuning, we propose a multi-task GNN readout architecture with task-specific multilayer perceptron heads, preserving shared representation learning while mitigating negative transfer and improving the modeling of heterogeneous, nonlinear task relationships. Across Biogen, ExpansionRX, and ChEMBL-MT, the resulting Contrastive KERMT pretraining improves over the KERMT baseline by 7.6%, 9.9%, and 9.5% respectively (averaged over significantly-improved endpoints). Adding ADME-adjacent molecules to the pretraining corpus further improves transfer, and the contrastive component sharpens chemically meaningful latent neighborhoods.

AI.tldr: A molecular graph-transformer pretraining framework combining chemistry-specific self-supervision with contrastive mutual information learning improves ADME property prediction by 7.6-9.9% over baselines across multiple datasets.
AI.motivation: Accurate prediction of ADME (absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion) properties is critical but challenging in drug discovery due to noisy, interdependent, and data-limited endpoints.
AI.method: Proposes a molecular graph-transformer pretraining framework combining chemistry-specific self-supervision with contrastive mutual information (cMIM). Encodes molecular graphs into latent variables, reconstructs SMILES strings, augments contrastive objective with domain-specific self-supervised chemistry tasks, formulated as unit-weighted log-probability factors in a single probabilistic objective. Uses multi-task GNN readout architecture with task-specific MLP heads for fine-tuning.
AI.result: The Contrastive KERMT pretraining improves over KERMT baseline by 7.6% (Biogen), 9.9% (ExpansionRX), and 9.5% (ChEMBL-MT) averaged over significantly-improved endpoints. Adding ADME-adjacent molecules to pretraining corpus further improves transfer, and contrastive component sharpens chemically meaningful latent neighborhoods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses challenges in ADME prediction through unified probabilistic pretraining and multi-task fine-tuning, demonstrating substantial improvements across multiple pharmaceutical datasets while learning chemically meaningful representations.

title: A Five-Plane Reference Architecture for Runtime Governance of Production AI Agents
comment: 65 pages, 3 figures, 5 tables. Reference architecture with a reference implementation of the policy-engine core and microbenchmark results; full-system evaluation identified as future work
summary:
Enterprise security was built to govern data boundaries: the protected surface was data at rest and in transit, and the controls -- access control, data-loss prevention, perimeter inspection -- governed crossings of that boundary. Production AI agents dissolve this assumption. An agent reads context, calls tools, invokes connectors, and modifies systems of record on an enterprise's behalf, so risk moves inside the workflow, into sequences of individually-permitted actions that may transform a business process no one authorized. Existing policy engines do not extend to this regime: they evaluate request-time decisions against atomic principals, where agentic systems require stateful evaluation against composite principals whose authority attenuates through delegation chains.
  We present a reference architecture for the runtime governance of production agents, built from four composable primitives: a five-plane decomposition (a reasoning plane that adjudicates intent, and four enforcement planes -- network, identity, endpoint, data -- that realize the decision), stop-anywhere mediation, composite principals with capability attenuation, and audit as a structured evidence substrate. We define a taxonomy of six interruption primitives that generalize allow and deny, state and argue for four correctness invariants, and demonstrate the foreclosure of seven production-agent threats across five concrete workflows. A reference implementation of the policy-engine core supplies measured evidence: attenuation correctness and evidence reconstructability hold on every trial, adjudication runs in single-digit microseconds, and the audit substrate's tamper-evidence behaves exactly as designed. We are explicit about scope: the architecture governs delegated action, not model behavior, and a full-system evaluation against a live agent benchmark is the invited next step.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a runtime governance architecture for production AI agents, addressing how traditional security controls fail for agentic workflows where risk moves inside sequences of individually-permitted actions.
AI.motivation: Traditional enterprise security assumes data boundaries and static permissions, but production AI agents dissolve this by performing sequences of actions on behalf of enterprises. Existing policy engines can't handle stateful evaluation against composite principals with delegation chains, creating security gaps for agentic systems.
AI.method: Proposes a reference architecture with four composable primitives: 1) five-plane decomposition (reasoning plane plus four enforcement planes), 2) stop-anywhere mediation, 3) composite principals with capability attenuation, and 4) audit as structured evidence substrate. Defines six interruption primitives and four correctness invariants.
AI.result: Demonstrates foreclosure of seven production-agent threats across five concrete workflows. Reference implementation shows: attenuation correctness and evidence reconstructability hold on every trial, adjudication runs in single-digit microseconds, and audit substrate tamper-evidence works as designed.
AI.conclusion: The architecture addresses governance of delegated action in AI agents, though scope is limited to action governance (not model behavior). A full-system evaluation against live agent benchmarks is needed as next step.

title: SirenFNO: Efficient and Full Frequency Learning of Fourier Neural Operators
summary: Fourier neural operators (FNOs) are effective and efficient surrogates for approximating solutions of PDEs and generalize across discretizations. However, owing to the reliance on frequency truncation to maintain learning efficiency of FNOs, empirical studies suggest that FNOs exhibit spectral bias toward low-frequency information, which may hinder the learning capability especially for certain PDEs with strong high-frequency oscillations. To address this limitation, we propose SirenFNO, a novel framework that leverages sinusoidal representation networks (SIRENs) to learn implicit neural representations and performs mode-wise kernel parameterization. Our SIREN parameterization learns a full-grid spectrum with a constant and discretization-independent parameter count, thereby eliminating the need for frequency truncation. We further extend SirenFNO with functional tensor decompositions to enhance parameter and learning efficiency. Empirical results show that our SirenFNO consistently outperforms FNO with approximately $4$ to $15$ times parameter reductions with preserved discretization invariance, and our functional decomposition variants obtain performance improvements with a maximum of $73$ times fewer parameters across multiple PDE benchmarks.

AI.tldr: SirenFNO is a novel framework that combines sinusoidal representation networks with Fourier neural operators to overcome spectral bias limitations, achieving better performance with significantly fewer parameters while maintaining discretization invariance.
AI.motivation: Standard Fourier neural operators (FNOs) exhibit spectral bias toward low-frequency information due to frequency truncation needed for learning efficiency. This limitation hinders their ability to learn PDEs with strong high-frequency oscillations.
AI.method: The authors propose SirenFNO, which integrates sinusoidal representation networks (SIRENs) to learn implicit neural representations and performs mode-wise kernel parameterization. This approach learns a full-grid spectrum with constant, discretization-independent parameters, eliminating frequency truncation. They further extend the framework with functional tensor decompositions to enhance efficiency.
AI.result: SirenFNO consistently outperforms standard FNO with approximately 4-15x parameter reductions while preserving discretization invariance. The functional decomposition variants achieve performance improvements with up to 73x fewer parameters across multiple PDE benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: By integrating SIRENs into the FNO framework, the proposed method successfully addresses spectral bias limitations, achieves superior performance with substantially reduced parameter counts, and maintains the desirable discretization invariance property of FNOs for PDE approximation tasks.

title: PROJECTMEM: A Local-First, Event-Sourced Memory and Judgment Layer for AI Coding Agents
summary: AI coding assistants now support a growing share of software work, from quick scripts to production applications. Yet these agents remain largely stateless: each new session re-reads project files, re-derives prior decisions, and - most costly - may repeat debugging attempts that already failed. Reconstructing this context can consume an estimated 5,000-20,000 tokens per session; the bottleneck is often not model capability but missing project memory. We present projectmem, an open-source, local-first memory and judgment layer for AI coding agents. projectmem records development as an append-only, plain-text event log of typed events - issues, attempts, fixes, decisions, and notes - and deterministically projects that log into compact, AI-readable summaries served through the Model Context Protocol (MCP). Beyond storage, projectmem adds a deterministic pre-action gate that warns an agent before it repeats a previously failed fix or edits a known-fragile file. We frame this as Memory-as-Governance: memory that does not merely answer the agent but acts on its next action. The system runs fully offline with no telemetry; its immutable log also serves as a provenance trail for reproducible, auditable AI-assisted development. projectmem ships as a three-dependency Python package (14 MCP tools, 19 CLI commands, 37 automated tests) and is evaluated through a two-month self-study across 10 projects comprising 207 logged events. Source code: https://github.com/riponcm/projectmem.

AI.tldr: projectmem is an open-source, local-first memory layer for AI coding agents that logs development events and provides AI-readable summaries to prevent repetitive debugging attempts and improve efficiency.
AI.motivation: Current AI coding assistants are stateless and waste significant tokens (5,000-20,000 per session) re-reading files, re-deriving decisions, and repeating failed debugging attempts. The bottleneck is missing project memory rather than model capability.
AI.method: Developed projectmem as a Python package with an append-only plain-text event log that records development events (issues, attempts, fixes, decisions, notes). It deterministically projects logs into compact AI-readable summaries served through Model Context Protocol (MCP), includes a deterministic pre-action gate to warn agents before repeating failed fixes or editing fragile files.
AI.result: Built a functional system with 14 MCP tools, 19 CLI commands, and 37 automated tests. Evaluated through two-month self-study across 10 projects comprising 207 logged events. The system runs fully offline with no telemetry and provides immutable logs for provenance.
AI.conclusion: projectmem addresses the memory gap in AI coding assistants through Memory-as-Governance, where memory not only answers agents but also acts on their next actions. It enables reproducible, auditable AI-assisted development while reducing token waste from repeated context reconstruction.

title: Counterexample Guided Learning in the Large using Reasoning Agents
comment: Code, data, and resources are publicly available for research purposes: https://github.com/Lhtie/CEGML
summary: LLMs and LLM agents should improve when given feedback, but identifying when they are able to do so is difficult: feedback is heterogeneous, domain-specific, and difficult to control. We approach this challenge by asking LLMs to perform regular-expression induction, a classical symbolic learning problem where precise mechanisms for feedback exist in the form of counterexamples. In counterexample-guided learning, a learner (LLM) proposes candidate regular expressions from positive/negative-labeled strings, and the teacher (verifier) returns counterexamples showcasing the difference between the candidate and target languages. We identify novel counterexample-guided refinement strategies that enable effective regex learning, such as regularization and symbolic counterexample clusters. We also explore agentic strategies such as reflection and repair loops. Empirically, we find that verifier feedback substantially improves sample efficiency on challenging regex-induction tasks, reducing the number of labeled examples required and enabling learning of complex target expressions where standard prompting fails. For example, on the hardest task groups, our counterexample-guided framework improves success from 3.2% to 38.1% and from 38.9% to 74.1% on two different regex domains. These results suggest that LLMs can benefit from rich feedback beyond treating it as additional data, opening the door for robust verifier-guided methods for LLM-based program synthesis and formal reasoning.

AI.tldr: Counterexample-guided learning enables LLMs to learn regular expressions more effectively by using verifier feedback with novel refinement strategies, improving success rates from 3.2% to 38.1% and 38.9% to 74.1% on challenging tasks.
AI.motivation: LLMs and LLM agents should improve when given feedback, but identifying when they can effectively use feedback is difficult due to feedback being heterogeneous, domain-specific, and hard to control. The paper addresses this by using regular-expression induction as a testbed where precise feedback mechanisms exist in the form of counterexamples.
AI.method: The approach uses counterexample-guided learning for regular expression induction: an LLM proposes candidate regular expressions from positive/negative-labeled strings, and a verifier returns counterexamples highlighting differences between candidate and target languages. Novel refinement strategies include regularization and symbolic counterexample clusters, along with agentic strategies like reflection and repair loops.
AI.result: Verifier feedback substantially improves sample efficiency on challenging regex-induction tasks, reducing required labeled examples and enabling learning of complex target expressions where standard prompting fails. Specifically, on the hardest task groups, success improved from 3.2% to 38.1% and from 38.9% to 74.1% across two different regex domains.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can benefit from rich feedback beyond treating it as additional data, opening the door for robust verifier-guided methods for LLM-based program synthesis and formal reasoning. The counterexample-guided framework demonstrates significant improvements in learning complex symbolic patterns.

title: Nonslop: A Gamified Experiment in Human-AI Collaborative Writing
comment: Accepted at the 2026 IEEE Conference on Games (CoG 2026); to be published in the conference proceedings. Camera-ready version
summary:
The rapid proliferation of large language models (LLMs) raises critical questions about human creativity and individual expression in an era of AI-assisted creation. When do humans adopt AI suggestions, and what are the implications for individual voice?
  This study examines these questions through a gamified writing exercise where 74 participants (214 responses) replied to prompts while AI-generated word suggestions were available as they wrote. The game simulates a dystopian future in which an AI is attempting to learn from what remains of human individuality, and disincentivizes AI-like writing. In doing so, it attempts to create conditions that reveal authentic user preferences rather than default behaviors, such as accepting a readily available AI-generated suggestion. Note that this is a deliberate inversion of the "helpful assistant" design pattern; the system is explicitly forbidding you from accepting AI suggestions.
  We analyze user behavior patterns across different task types, user behaviors, and response characteristics to understand the factors influencing human-AI interaction in creative tasks. The study focuses on when users choose to maintain creative autonomy versus violating the rules of the game and accepting AI assistance. It also explores how these choices relate to response patterns, task characteristics, and user behavior. This gamified approach offers both a framework for studying authentic human-AI interaction and a provocative lens for understanding the tension between efficiency and authenticity in AI-augmented creativity.

AI.tldr: A gamified writing study investigates when humans adopt AI suggestions in creative tasks by simulating a dystopian future where AI suggestions are explicitly forbidden, revealing authentic user preferences about maintaining creative autonomy versus accepting AI assistance.
AI.motivation: The rapid proliferation of large language models raises critical questions about human creativity and individual expression in AI-assisted creation. Researchers want to understand when humans adopt AI suggestions and the implications for individual voice in creative work.
AI.method: Conducted a gamified writing exercise with 74 participants (214 responses) where they replied to prompts with AI-generated word suggestions available. The game simulates a dystopian future where AI attempts to learn from human individuality while explicitly forbidding acceptance of AI suggestions, creating conditions to reveal authentic user preferences.
AI.result: Analysis of user behavior patterns across different task types, behaviors, and response characteristics to understand factors influencing human-AI interaction in creative tasks. Focuses on when users maintain creative autonomy versus violate rules to accept AI assistance, and how these choices relate to response patterns, task characteristics, and user behavior.
AI.conclusion: This gamified approach provides both a framework for studying authentic human-AI interaction and a provocative lens for understanding the tension between efficiency and authenticity in AI-augmented creativity. The study offers insights into creative autonomy in AI-assisted environments.

title: APEX: A Network-Native Time-Series Foundation Model for Forecasting and Anomaly Detection for Wireless Edge Operations
comment: 5 pages, 1 figure, 4 tables. Discusses a network-native time-series foundation model for wireless edge operations
summary: Generic time-series foundation models transfer poorly to wireless network telemetry whose signals are bursty, zero-inflated, and coupled across protocol layers. We present APEX, a network-native, decoder-only transformer for forecasting enterprise AP telemetry, and evaluate it on DHCP degradation as a representative network task. APEX is pre-trained on 10-channel multivariate telemetry from ~4,500 production wireless networks (~100K AP time series, 34 metrics per AP), and is available as APEX-Large (269M, cloud) and APEX-Edge (10.5M, edge). On a 192-step (4-day) DHCP degradation benchmark, APEX-Large reduces MAE by 18% over the strongest foundation-model baseline (Toto) and 38% over SARIMA, with anomaly-detection F1 = 0.93, while APEX-Edge enables sub-second, privacy-preserving inference on AP-class edge hardware. These results suggest network-native pre-training is a practical foundation for proactive wireless operations.

AI.tldr: APEX is a network-native transformer model for forecasting wireless AP telemetry that significantly outperforms existing foundation models on network-specific tasks like DHCP degradation prediction, with both cloud and edge deployment options.
AI.motivation: Generic time-series foundation models perform poorly on wireless network telemetry due to its unique characteristics: bursty signals, zero-inflation, and cross-protocol coupling. There's a need for specialized models that can handle these network-specific patterns for proactive wireless operations.
AI.method: APEX is a decoder-only transformer specifically designed for network telemetry. It was pre-trained on 10-channel multivariate telemetry from ~4,500 production wireless networks (~100K AP time series, 34 metrics per AP). Two versions were developed: APEX-Large (269M parameters) for cloud deployment and APEX-Edge (10.5M parameters) for edge hardware deployment.
AI.result: On a 192-step (4-day) DHCP degradation benchmark, APEX-Large reduced MAE by 18% over the strongest foundation-model baseline (Toto) and 38% over SARIMA, achieving anomaly-detection F1 = 0.93. APEX-Edge enabled sub-second, privacy-preserving inference on AP-class edge hardware.
AI.conclusion: Network-native pre-training is a practical foundation for proactive wireless operations, as demonstrated by APEX's superior performance on network-specific forecasting tasks compared to generic foundation models.

title: GraphInfer-Bench: Benchmarking LLM's Inference Capability on Graphs
summary: Graph analysis underlies many applications whose answers cannot be looked up in a single record or retrieved along a path: laundering rings, drug repurposing, user preference, and scientific theme are all inferred from a node together with its neighbourhood. We introduce GraphInfer-Bench, a benchmark for whether LLMs can perform this graph inference: producing an open-ended answer that no single node supports and no path retrieves. Existing graph-QA protocols cannot test this capability: algorithm simulation, node classification, single-node description, KG-QA, and GraphRAG all admit answers retrievable from one node or along a path. GraphInfer-Bench defines five tasks along Description (what a region is) and Comparison (how regions differ), each constructed so the ground truth lives in no single node. The release contains 42,000 samples across six real-world graphs, produced automatically and screened by a four-layer quality-control protocol. We evaluate four method families against the same tasks: graph-token alignment models, zero-shot frontier closed-source LLMs, Graph2Text supervised fine-tuning, and plain GNNs as a structural reference. No method family closes the gap. Graph-token alignment partially handles description tasks (relational, theme) but collapses on comparison tasks. Frontier LLMs lead on outlier detection and community partition among LLM-based methods but lag on masked-node prediction. Graph2Text SFT is the strongest LLM-based method on the description side yet falls behind frontier LLMs on comparison. Across every task, plain GNNs match or beat the strongest LLM-based row, with the largest margin on community detection. GraphInfer-Bench surfaces graph inference as an open capability gap rather than a property of any one architecture.

AI.tldr: GraphInfer-Bench is a new benchmark designed to test LLMs' ability to perform true graph inference - producing answers not found in single nodes or paths, addressing capabilities like detecting laundering rings and drug repurposing. The benchmark includes 42,000 samples across 6 real graphs with 5 tasks, showing that current methods (graph-token alignment, frontier LLMs, Graph2Text SFT, plain GNNs) all fail to close the gap, with GNNs outperforming LLM-based methods.
AI.motivation: Existing graph-QA protocols cannot test true graph inference capability because they admit answers retrievable from single nodes or paths. Many real-world applications like laundering ring detection, drug repurposing, user preference analysis, and scientific theme identification require inferring answers from a node together with its neighborhood - producing open-ended answers that no single node supports and no path retrieves.
AI.method: The authors create GraphInfer-Bench with five tasks along Description (what a region is) and Comparison (how regions differ) axes, each constructed so ground truth lives in no single node. They use 42,000 samples across six real-world graphs, produced automatically and screened by a four-layer quality-control protocol. They evaluate four method families: graph-token alignment models, zero-shot frontier closed-source LLMs, Graph2Text supervised fine-tuning, and plain GNNs as a structural reference.
AI.result: No method family closes the capability gap. Graph-token alignment partially handles description tasks but collapses on comparison tasks. Frontier LLMs lead on outlier detection and community partition among LLM-based methods but lag on masked-node prediction. Graph2Text SFT is strongest on description tasks among LLMs but falls behind frontier LLMs on comparison. Plain GNNs match or beat the strongest LLM-based method across every task, with the largest margin on community detection.
AI.conclusion: GraphInfer-Bench surfaces graph inference as an open capability gap rather than a property of any one architecture. Current methods, including the most advanced LLM approaches, cannot adequately perform true graph inference as defined by tasks requiring answers not retrievable from single nodes or paths. This reveals a fundamental limitation in existing AI architectures for graph reasoning tasks.

title: Range-Aware Bayesian Optimization for Discovering Diverse Designs within Target Property Windows
comment: 64 pages, 6 main text figures, 17 supporting figures, 6 supporting tables
summary: In many materials and product design problems, desirable candidates exhibit properties that fall within an acceptable range rather than achieve a single optimum. Recovering multiple, distinct solutions that satisfy such specifications is also practically valuable, as some candidates may be preferred for reasons of cost, processability, or robustness that are difficult to encode directly in an objective function. Here, we develop a range-aware Bayesian optimization (BO) framework in which the acquisition function directly scores the posterior probability that a candidate satisfies a target range. The framework naturally extends to parallel pursuit of multiple distinct specifications over a shared candidate space. Across benchmark tasks, range-aware acquisition consistently recovers larger and more diverse sets of valid designs than standard BO baselines and recent goal-seeking methods. Its utility is further demonstrated in two practically motivated design case studies involving optimizing reaction conditions for polymer synthesis and sequence-defined oligomer discovery for prescribed optical absorption bands, supported by quantum chemical calculations. These results suggest that range-aware BO can provide a practical and sample-efficient foundation for specification-driven design, particularly when design flexibility and solution diversity are important considerations.

AI.tldr: A Bayesian optimization framework called range-aware BO that finds multiple distinct solutions within specified property ranges, outperforming standard BO methods for specification-driven design problems.
AI.motivation: Many materials and product design problems require candidates with properties falling within acceptable ranges rather than single optimum values. Practical needs call for discovering multiple distinct solutions that satisfy specifications, as different candidates may be preferred for cost, processability, or robustness reasons that are hard to encode in objective functions.
AI.method: Develops a range-aware Bayesian optimization framework where the acquisition function directly scores the posterior probability that a candidate satisfies a target property range. Extends to parallel pursuit of multiple distinct specifications over a shared candidate space.
AI.result: Across benchmark tasks, range-aware acquisition consistently recovers larger and more diverse sets of valid designs than standard BO baselines and recent goal-seeking methods. Demonstrated utility in two practical design case studies: optimizing reaction conditions for polymer synthesis, and sequence-defined oligomer discovery for prescribed optical absorption bands using quantum chemical calculations.
AI.conclusion: Range-aware BO provides a practical and sample-efficient foundation for specification-driven design, particularly when design flexibility and solution diversity are important considerations.

title: Beyond the Golden Teacher: Enhancing Graph Learning through LLM-GNN Co-teaching
summary: Text-attributed graphs (TAGs) underlie real-world applications such as citation networks, social media, and e-commerce. Few-shot graph learning on TAGs is hard: with only a handful of labels per class and the rest of the graph unannotated, neither GNNs nor LLMs can learn well on their own. GNNs read topology and fail on cold nodes; LLMs read text and fail on text-ambiguous nodes. Existing LLM-GNN methods all follow the same recipe: designate one model as the golden teacher and use its outputs (e.g., features or pseudo-labels) to supervise the other. We argue this golden-teacher assumption breaks under sparse supervision: neither model is golden, and treating either as such transfers its blind spots into the student. We therefore ask: can we avoid designating either model as the golden teacher, and still perform effective graph learning?
