We answer with LLM-GNN Co-Teaching, a bidirectional co-teaching framework in which neither model is fixed as teacher. The GNN and LLM exchange their most confident pseudo-labels under an architecture-specific small-loss criterion, and both update every round. Supervision is then mined from the trajectory: whenever a node moves from cross-model contradiction at round t to cross-model agreement at round t+1, the LLM's two answers on the same input form a preference pair (old contradicting self < new peer-endorsed self) for DPO training. We call this Round-based Pseudo-Label Preference Optimization (RPL-PO). On six benchmarks, LLM-GNN Co-Teaching consistently outperforms GNN-as-Judge and all prior methods, with absolute 3-shot gains of 7.86% on Cora and 7.73% on ogbn-arxiv; improvements carry over to 5-shot and to zero-shot cross-dataset transfer. Error-structure analysis further shows that abandoning the golden-teacher assumption substantially improves the LLM's graph learning capability on challenging samples.

AI.tldr: LLM-GNN Co-Teaching: A bidirectional co-teaching framework for few-shot graph learning that avoids designating either model as the golden teacher, instead having LLMs and GNNs exchange confident pseudo-labels and mine supervision from their learning trajectories.
AI.motivation: Few-shot learning on text-attributed graphs is challenging because neither GNNs (which struggle with cold nodes) nor LLMs (which struggle with text-ambiguous nodes) can learn well alone with sparse supervision. Existing methods fail by treating one model as a "golden teacher," transferring its blind spots to the other model.
AI.method: Proposes LLM-GNN Co-Teaching framework where GNN and LLM exchange their most confident pseudo-labels using an architecture-specific small-loss criterion, with both models updating every round. Introduces Round-based Pseudo-Label Preference Optimization (RPL-PO) where supervision is mined from learning trajectories: when a node moves from cross-model contradiction to agreement, the LLM's answers form preference pairs for DPO training.
AI.result: On six benchmarks, LLM-GNN Co-Teaching consistently outperforms GNN-as-Judge and all prior methods, achieving absolute 3-shot gains of 7.86% on Cora and 7.73% on ogbn-arxiv. Improvements extend to 5-shot and zero-shot cross-dataset transfer. Error analysis shows substantial improvement on challenging samples.
AI.conclusion: Abandoning the golden-teacher assumption enables more effective few-shot graph learning by allowing LLMs and GNNs to co-teach each other bidirectionally, leveraging their complementary strengths while avoiding transferring blind spots, leading to significant performance gains on text-attributed graphs.

title: Kuramoto Attention: Synchronizing Self-Attention on the Torus
summary: We introduce Kuramoto attention, a self-attention layer in which each hidden coordinate is an angle. The layer scores tokens by gated cosine similarity, attends over previous phase states, and updates each token by the tangent component of the attention-weighted circular mean. Because the values are the raw phase states, this update is exactly the Kuramoto coupling term $\sum_u A_{t,u}\sin(θ_u-θ_t)$, with the attention matrix acting as an adaptive, content-dependent coupling kernel. Equivalently, the gated score is a learned metric on the torus that selects which tokens couple, and the update pulls each token toward the circular mean of the tokens it selects, tightening their phase agreement. The same two ingredients, an invariant similarity score and an on-manifold mean, define such a layer on any compact group; the torus is the abelian case, where both are closed-form. The softmax weights solve an entropy-regularized phase-retrieval problem, and rotary position enters as a position-dependent phase drift in the score. On enwiki8 character-level language modeling, the layer trains as a functional language model whose bits-per-character stays close to a strong matched RoPE+SwiGLU transformer: within $0.02$ BPC at one million parameters ($1.637\pm0.010$ versus $1.616\pm0.004$) and level on the median at five million ($1.448$ versus $1.452$ over five seeds) with the transformer ahead on the mean ($1.468$ versus $1.456$). These experiments establish that the constrained geometric structure is a viable language model at this scale; the structure itself, and its synchronization reading, is the contribution. Ablations isolate the load-bearing components, and the result gives a compact bridge between self-attention and phase synchronization.

AI.tldr: Kuramoto attention replaces conventional self-attention with angular coordinates, using gated cosine similarity for scoring and Kuramoto coupling for updates, achieving comparable performance to standard transformers on language modeling tasks.
AI.motivation: To explore constrained geometric structure in attention mechanisms and bridge self-attention with phase synchronization, while maintaining competitive performance in language modeling.
AI.method: Self-attention layer with angular hidden coordinates, using gated cosine similarity for token scoring and Kuramoto coupling term for updates, where attention matrix acts as adaptive content-dependent coupling kernel.
AI.result: Kuramoto attention performs close to RoPE+SwiGLU transformers: within 0.02 BPC at 1M parameters (1.637 vs 1.616) and comparable at 5M parameters (1.448 vs 1.452 median), establishing viability of constrained geometric structure.
AI.conclusion: The geometric structure of Kuramoto attention is viable for language modeling at this scale, providing a compact bridge between self-attention and phase synchronization, with the structure itself and synchronization reading being the main contribution.

title: Physics-Distilled Neural Network enabled by Large Language Models for Manufacturing Process-Property Predictive Modeling
comment: Under review, Journal of Computing and Information Science in Engineering
summary: Predicting process-property relationships in manufacturing is often challenged by high experimental costs and the limited interpretability of complex 'black-box' models. This paper proposes a novel knowledge distillation framework designed to achieve high-accuracy predictions in data-scarce scenarios. The framework integrates analytical physics priors, which are systematically extracted from scientific literature via Large Language Models, into a privileged teacher model. We employ a Graph-Masked Attention layer to capture the complex physical dependencies among input variables showing strict setpoints or a combination of static and high-frequency temporal signatures. This privileged knowledge is distilled into a lightweight student predictor for inference. The feasibility and robustness of the framework are evaluated through a comprehensive experiment across five diverse manufacturing processes. To ensure statistical reliability, given the small dataset sizes, a repeated K-fold cross-validation technique is employed to quantify model stability and generalization. Results indicate that the proposed framework consistently achieves high predictive accuracy across all evaluated domains. Most importantly, the architecture demonstrates significant fault tolerance by maintaining robust predictive performance even in scenarios where LLM-derived analytical priors are suboptimal or incomplete. Furthermore, the student predictor achieves an inference frequency exceeding 6000 Hz, which facilitates real-time edge deployment on standard industrial hardware. This work provides a scalable solution for bridging the gap between theoretical physics and real-time industrial monitoring in data-limited environments.

AI.tldr: A knowledge distillation framework using physics priors from LLMs achieves high accuracy in data-scarce manufacturing process prediction while maintaining real-time inference capabilities.
AI.motivation: Predicting process-property relationships in manufacturing faces challenges of high experimental costs, limited data, and poor interpretability of black-box models in data-scarce environments.
AI.method: A knowledge distillation framework that extracts physics priors from scientific literature via LLMs, integrates them into a privileged teacher model using Graph-Masked Attention to capture physical dependencies, then distills knowledge into a lightweight student predictor.
AI.result: The framework achieves high predictive accuracy across five manufacturing domains, maintains robust performance with suboptimal LLM-derived priors, and achieves >6000 Hz inference frequency enabling real-time edge deployment.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a scalable solution bridging theoretical physics and real-time industrial monitoring in data-limited environments with fault tolerance and practical deployment capabilities.

title: Information-Theoretic Decomposition for Multimodal Interaction Learning
summary: Multimodal learning hinges on capturing redundant, unique, and synergistic information across modalities, which collectively constitute multimodal interactions. A critical yet underexplored challenge is that these implicit interactions vary dynamically across samples. In this work, we present the first systematic, information-theoretic analysis highlighting why learning these dynamic, sample-specific interactions is critical for effective multimodal learning. Our analysis further reveals deficits in conventional paradigms at learning these distinct interaction types: modality ensemble approaches struggle to capture synergy, while joint learning paradigms often under-utilize redundant information. This highlights the need for an approach that can adaptively learn from different interaction types on a per-sample basis. To this end, we propose Decomposition-based Multimodal Interaction Learning (DMIL), a novel paradigm that explicitly models and learns from sample-specific interactions. First, we design a variational decomposition architecture to isolate the constituent interaction components. Second, we employ a new learning strategy that leverages these explicit interaction components in a fine-tuning process to achieve comprehensive interaction learning. Extensive experiments across diverse tasks and architectures demonstrate that DMIL consistently achieves superior performance by adapting to holistic sample-specific interactions. Our framework is flexible and broadly applicable, establishing an interaction-centric paradigm for multimodal learning. The code is available at https://github.com/GeWu-Lab/DMIL.

AI.tldr: DMIL is a novel multimodal learning framework that explicitly models and learns from sample-specific interactions (redundant, unique, and synergistic information) through variational decomposition and fine-tuning, achieving superior performance across diverse tasks.
AI.motivation: Multimodal interactions vary dynamically across samples, and conventional paradigms fail to capture these sample-specific interactions: modality ensemble struggles with synergy, while joint learning under-utilizes redundancy.
AI.method: DMIL uses a variational decomposition architecture to isolate interaction components (redundant, unique, synergistic) followed by a learning strategy that leverages these explicit components in fine-tuning for comprehensive interaction learning.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across diverse tasks and architectures show DMIL consistently achieves superior performance by adapting to holistic sample-specific interactions.
AI.conclusion: DMIL establishes an interaction-centric paradigm for multimodal learning that is flexible, broadly applicable, and outperforms conventional approaches by explicitly learning sample-specific multimodal interactions.

title: DeMix: Debugging Training Data with Mixed Data Error Types by Investigating Influence Vectors
summary: High-quality training data is essential for the success of machine learning models. However, real-world datasets often contain mixed types of errors arising from systematic flaws in data preparation pipelines, including label errors, feature errors, and spurious correlations. Effective debugging of training data requires both detecting erroneous samples and identifying their specific error types to enable targeted repair, yet existing data cleaning and attribution methods fail to adequately address this dual requirement. In this paper, we propose DeMix, a novel framework that simultaneously diagnoses erroneous samples and their error types. Our key insight is that different error types produce distinct patterns on model behavior. DeMix captures such error-specific patterns by influence vectors that characterize how each training sample affects model predictions across all validation samples. We formulate training data debugging as a multi-label classification problem where a classifier is developed to predict error types directly from influence vectors. We further introduce an intervention-based learning strategy that guides the classifier to capture invariant rationales specific to each error type, ensuring the learned classifier generalizes effectively. Empirical evaluations on 11 tasks across tabular data prediction, recommendation systems, and LLM alignment demonstrate that DeMix significantly outperforms state-of-the-art approaches, achieving a 22.61% improvement in data debugging F1-score and a 9.32% gain in task model performance after data repair. Code is available at: https://github.com/SJTU-DMTai/DeMix.

AI.tldr: DeMix is a framework that simultaneously detects erroneous training samples and identifies their specific error types using influence vectors and intervention-based learning.
AI.motivation: Real-world datasets contain various errors (label errors, feature errors, spurious correlations) from flawed data pipelines, but existing methods fail to both detect errors and identify their specific types needed for targeted repair.
AI.method: DeMix uses influence vectors that capture how each training sample affects model predictions across validation samples. It formulates data debugging as multi-label classification predicting error types from influence vectors, with intervention-based learning to capture error-type-specific invariant rationales.
AI.result: DeMix achieves 22.61% improvement in data debugging F1-score and 9.32% gain in task model performance after data repair across 11 tasks in tabular data, recommendation systems, and LLM alignment.
AI.conclusion: DeMix effectively addresses the dual challenge of error detection and type identification by leveraging error-specific patterns in model behavior, enabling more targeted and effective data repair.

title: TimeRouter: Efficient and Adaptive Routing of Time-Series Foundation Models
summary: Time-series foundation models (TSFMs) are increasingly explored as predictive experts within emerging agentic time-series systems. However, TSFMs exhibit heterogeneous inductive biases, and no single model consistently dominates across forecasting regimes, making expert selection a critical challenge. Existing systems often delegate this decision to LLM-based controllers, incurring substantial inference overhead. We present TimeRouter, an efficient routing framework that leverages empirical complementarity across a pool of pretrained TSFMs through lightweight discriminative routing, selective gating, and ensemble fallback. Concretely, TimeRouter combines a learned routing head, a selective gate, and an ensemble fallback, enabling adaptive expert selection without invoking an LLM at inference time. TimeRouter achieves state-of-the-art performance on the GIFT-EVAL leaderboard, with an LB MASE of 0.6765. Beyond benchmark performance, our ablation studies provide empirical insights into TSFM routing design, highlighting the importance of pool composition and selective gating. Taken together, these results position TimeRouter as a modular and lightweight routing layer for future agentic time-series systems built upon foundation-model pools. Our code is available at https://github.com/UConn-DSIS/TimeRouter.

AI.tldr: TimeRouter is an efficient routing framework that automatically selects the best time-series foundation model from a pool for each forecasting task, avoiding LLM-based controllers and achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Time-series foundation models (TSFMs) have varying strengths and no single model performs best across all forecasting scenarios. Current systems use LLM-based controllers for expert selection, which incurs significant inference overhead and computational cost.
AI.method: TimeRouter uses lightweight discriminative routing, selective gating, and ensemble fallback to efficiently select the most appropriate TSFM from a pool. It includes a learned routing head, selective gate, and ensemble fallback mechanism that operates without LLM invocation during inference.
AI.result: TimeRouter achieves state-of-the-art performance on the GIFT-EVAL leaderboard with an LB MASE of 0.6765. Ablation studies provide insights into TSFM routing design, showing the importance of pool composition and selective gating.
AI.conclusion: TimeRouter provides a modular and lightweight routing layer for agentic time-series systems, enabling efficient expert selection from foundation model pools without LLM overhead, while maintaining top performance.

title: When Context Returns: Toward Robust Internalization in On-Policy Distillation
summary: Recent work has shown that on-policy distillation can internalize privileged context, such as system prompts or task hints, into a student model so that the context is no longer needed at inference time. Although this approach successfully improves the student's no-context performance, we identify an interesting and previously unstudied phenomenon: in many settings, reintroducing the original privileged context to the distilled student actually degrades its performance, even on instances it already solves correctly without context. We term this context-induced degradation and argue that robust internalization demands not only matching the teacher's context-conditioned behavior, but also remaining stable when the context is reintroduced, a property we call context removability. Motivated by this observation, we propose a lightweight consistency regularizer that first anchors the student's no-context output via stop-gradient, then penalizes the context-conditioned output for deviating from it via forward KL divergence. This simple addition requires only one extra forward pass per training step, yet it effectively mitigates context-induced degradation and, in many cases, even improves no-context performance. Across 12 configurations spanning diverse domains and model families, our method improves context-conditioned accuracy in the majority of settings, reduces context-induced harm in 11 out of 12 settings, and effectively eliminates response-length inflation. A mechanistic case study further confirms that context removability is achieved at the representation level, with hidden states remaining nearly identical regardless of whether the context is present.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies "context-induced degradation" - a phenomenon where reintroducing privileged context to a distilled student model actually worsens its performance. The authors propose a lightweight consistency regularization method to ensure "context removability," improving model stability when context is added or removed.
AI.motivation: The authors discovered an unexpected problem in on-policy distillation: when privileged context (like system prompts or task hints) is reintroduced to a distilled student model that no longer needs it, the model's performance degrades even on instances it already solves correctly without context. This "context-induced degradation" reveals that robust internalization requires not just matching teacher behavior but also maintaining stability when context is reintroduced (context removability).
AI.method: The authors propose a lightweight consistency regularizer that: 1) anchors the student's no-context output using stop-gradient, and 2) penalizes the context-conditioned output for deviating from the anchored output via forward KL divergence. This requires only one extra forward pass per training step and aims to ensure the model behaves consistently regardless of context presence.
AI.result: Across 12 diverse configurations spanning different domains and model families, their method: improves context-conditioned accuracy in most settings, reduces context-induced harm in 11 out of 12 settings, effectively eliminates response-length inflation. A mechanistic study confirms context removability is achieved at the representation level, with hidden states remaining nearly identical whether context is present or not.
AI.conclusion: Context removability is an important property for robust knowledge internalization in distillation. The proposed consistency regularization effectively addresses context-induced degradation while improving model performance, demonstrating that stability under context reintroduction is crucial for successful distillation.

title: TAROT: Task-Adaptive Refinement of LLM-prior Graphs for Few-shot Tabular Learning
summary: Few-shot tabular learning provides a cost-effective approach for real-world applications where annotation is costly and collecting sufficient samples for new tasks is difficult. Existing Traditional and LLM-based methods have demonstrated effectiveness in few-shot scenarios. However, traditional methods need additional training on unlabeled or generated data, which incur significant computational overhead. In addition, LLM-based methods that directly feed raw tabular data into LLMs raise privacy and compliance concerns. More importantly, both paradigms largely overlook the semantic relationships between features, which provide structural and semantic prior for constructing a semantic graph. Semantic graph is essential for modeling meaningful feature interactions in few-shot scenarios. In this paper, we propose TAROT, a GNN-based framework that encodes the structural and semantic prior by constructing and refining a task-adaptive semantic graph from this prior, thereby improving predictive performance in few-shot tabular learning. TAROT first encodes heterogeneous tabular data into unified node semantic representations via a Unified Semantic Tabular Node Encoder (USTNE). Then, it prompts LLMs to infer the semantic relationship between features based on the task description and feature names to construct a semantic graph. To mitigate structural noise introduced by the hallucination of LLMs, TAROT introduces Task-adaptive Semantic Graph Refinement that prunes spurious or task-unrelated edges and adds missing task-related ones, aligning the graph structure with the downstream objective. Finally, a GNN performs message passing over the refined graph to capture task-related semantic dependencies for prediction. Extensive experiments on various few-shot tabular learning benchmarks demonstrate the superior performance of TAROT, establishing it as a state-of-the-art approach in this domain.

AI.tldr: TAROT is a GNN-based framework for few-shot tabular learning that constructs a task-adaptive semantic graph by leveraging semantic relationships between features, addressing limitations of traditional and LLM-based methods.
AI.motivation: Current few-shot tabular learning methods have several issues: traditional methods require expensive additional training on unlabeled/generated data; LLM-based methods pose privacy risks when feeding raw data into models; and both overlook semantic relationships between features, which provide important structural and semantic priors for building semantic graphs.
AI.method: TAROT has three main components: 1) Unified Semantic Tabular Node Encoder (USTNE) to encode heterogeneous tabular data into unified node representations; 2) LLM prompting to infer semantic relationships between features using task descriptions and feature names; 3) Task-adaptive Semantic Graph Refinement to prune spurious edges and add missing task-related edges, mitigating LLM hallucination effects.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on various few-shot tabular learning benchmarks demonstrate superior performance, establishing TAROT as a state-of-the-art approach in the domain.
AI.conclusion: TAROT effectively addresses limitations of existing few-shot tabular learning methods by constructing task-adaptive semantic graphs that capture meaningful feature interactions while maintaining computational efficiency and privacy compliance.

title: Tree-Structured Orthonormal Decomposition of the Aitchison Simplex
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026. To appear in PMLR vol. 306
summary: Compositional data -- vectors encoding relative proportions -- arise across scientific domains, including ecology, geochemistry, and genomics. The features in these data often come with known hierarchical structure (e.g., taxonomies, phylogenies, ontologies), yet existing methods either ignore this structure, discard the intrinsic Aitchison geometry, are designed for binary trees, or yield incomplete coordinate systems. We describe PolyILR, a canonical orthonormal decomposition of the Aitchison tangent space aligned with any tree topology. Our construction defines a weighted local geometry at each internal node capturing full branching structure, then lifts these to a global orthonormal basis where every coordinate corresponds to a specific tree location. On microbiome and single-cell benchmarks, PolyILR yields stable, interpretable features and enables inference at multiscale tree resolution. We also establish a novel theoretical connection to softmax classifiers, suggesting possible applications to probabilistic modeling.

AI.tldr: PolyILR provides a canonical orthonormal decomposition of Aitchison tangent space aligned with any tree topology, enabling compositional data analysis while preserving hierarchical structure and geometry.
AI.motivation: Compositional data with known hierarchical structures (taxonomies, phylogenies, ontologies) are common across scientific domains, but existing methods either ignore the hierarchical structure, discard Aitchison geometry, are limited to binary trees, or produce incomplete coordinate systems.
AI.method: PolyILR constructs a weighted local geometry at each internal node capturing full branching structure, then lifts these to a global orthonormal basis where every coordinate corresponds to a specific tree location, providing a canonical orthonormal decomposition of the Aitchison tangent space.
AI.result: On microbiome and single-cell benchmarks, PolyILR yields stable, interpretable features and enables inference at multiscale tree resolution. It also establishes a novel theoretical connection to softmax classifiers.
AI.conclusion: PolyILR provides a principled method for analyzing compositional data with hierarchical structure while preserving both the Aitchison geometry and tree topology, with potential applications to probabilistic modeling through its connection to softmax classifiers.

title: Structure-Preserving Neural Surrogates with Tractable Uncertainty Quantification
summary: Recent advances in scientific machine learning provide a means of near-real-time solution to partial differential equations (PDEs), but lack the theoretical underpinnings of conventional simulators that support contemporary verification and validation. In this work, we construct data-driven reduced-order models that serve as structure-preserving, real-time surrogates. Remarkably, the exterior calculus that imposes physical conservation structure also exposes topological structure that we use to build a Gaussian process (GP) representation of uncertainty in state-flux relationships, ultimately yielding a Dirichlet-to-Neumann map for quantities of interest with closed-form expressions for posterior uncertainty. We specifically propose structure-preserving $H(\mathrm{div})$--$L^2$ subspaces of conventional Raviart--Thomas and $dgP_0$ elements prescribed by a lightweight transformer. Reduced-order dynamics consistent with this subspace are learned by posing a conservation law in which a GP describes the fluxes between volumes. This work hinges on a novel interface between mixed FEM spaces and GP regression; when training is posed as the optimal recovery problem (ORP), the resulting GP regression can be written as an optimization problem with equality constraints that impose a conservation structure, amenable to a fast Schur-complement training strategy. The trained model can then be solved in real time with closed-form estimators for boundary fluxes driven by prescribed Dirichlet data. The paper includes RKHS posterior error bounds for linear functionals to support uncertainty quantification, as well as numerical experiments demonstrating the accuracy of the posterior distribution as a surrogate for error estimation.

AI.tldr: A structure-preserving reduced-order model that combines mixed FEM spaces with Gaussian processes for real-time PDE solution with uncertainty quantification.
AI.motivation: Scientific ML provides near-real-time PDE solutions but lacks theoretical underpinnings for verification/validation. Need structure-preserving surrogates with uncertainty quantification.
AI.method: Construct data-driven reduced-order models using H(div)-L² subspaces of Raviart-Thomas and dgP₀ elements via lightweight transformer. Learn reduced-order dynamics with GP regression as optimal recovery problem, imposing conservation structure through equality constraints. Use Schur-complement training strategy.
AI.result: Developed novel interface between mixed FEM spaces and GP regression. Obtains Dirichlet-to-Neumann map with closed-form posterior uncertainty expressions. Provides real-time solutions with boundary flux estimators and RKHS posterior error bounds for uncertainty quantification.
AI.conclusion: The method bridges scientific ML with theoretical foundations, providing structure-preserving, real-time surrogates with rigorous uncertainty quantification for PDE verification/validation.

title: DeepRHP: A Hybrid Variational Autoencoder for Designing Random Heteropolymers as Protein Mimics
comment: Oral presentation at AAAI 2023 Workshop on AI to Accelerate Science and Engineering
summary: Synthetic random heteropolymers (RHPs), consisting of a predefined set of monomers, offer an approach toward the design of protein-like materials. These RHPs, if designed appropriately, can mimic protein behavior and function. As such, there is a need for computational tools to efficiently guide RHP design. We bridge this gap by developing DeepRHP, a modified variational autoencoder (VAE) model under a semi-supervised framework. By equipping a classical VAE with an additional feature-based VAE, DeepRHP forces the latent space to capture structures of critical chemical features as well as individual RHP sequence patterns. In this sense, our method is versatile by allowing any relevant features to be incorporated in a hybrid manner. We demonstrate the effectiveness of DeepRHP by suggesting potential monomer compositions that stabilize membrane proteins (e.g. Aquaporin Z) in non-native environments and cross-validating our prediction with published results. The concordance between our model and true RHP function suggests strong potential in utilizing hybrid autoencoder architectures to guide RHP design for proteins and other biological compounds.

AI.tldr: DeepRHP is a modified variational autoencoder that combines sequence and chemical feature information to guide the design of synthetic random heteropolymers that can stabilize membrane proteins.
AI.motivation: Synthetic random heteropolymers (RHPs) can mimic protein behavior but lack computational tools for efficient design. The paper aims to bridge this gap by developing a computational method to guide RHP design.
AI.method: DeepRHP uses a modified variational autoencoder (VAE) under a semi-supervised framework with an additional feature-based VAE component. This hybrid architecture forces the latent space to capture both critical chemical features and individual RHP sequence patterns, allowing versatile incorporation of relevant features.
AI.result: The method successfully suggested potential monomer compositions that stabilize membrane proteins (Aquaporin Z) in non-native environments. Cross-validation with published results showed strong concordance between model predictions and true RHP function.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid autoencoder architecture shows strong potential for guiding RHP design for proteins and other biological compounds, as evidenced by the agreement between model predictions and experimental results.

title: IAPO: Input Attribution-Aware Policy Optimization for Tool Use in Small Multimodal Agents
summary: This paper investigates reinforcement learning (RL) methods for improving tool-calling capabilities in multimodal small language model (SLM) agents. While existing works have explored various reward designs to improve agentic tool-calling ability, these approaches face inherent limitations for SLM training, especially under multimodal scenarios. First, many existing methods evaluate tool use correctness through exact matching against certain ground-truth or predefined formats. However, this assumption is often unsuitable for multimodal tasks, where multiple tool use paths may be valid and annotated tool trajectories are typically unavailable. Second, such sparse and brittle binary rewards provide little guidance on how to improve the underlying decision process, making them particularly difficult for multimodal SLM to learn from. To address these issues, we propose Input Attribution-Aware Policy Optimization (IAPO), an RL algorithm for improving tool use in multimodal SLM by aligning the model's attribution across input components with that of a stronger teacher. Experiments on Qwen2.5-VL-3B show that the proposed method improves visual question answering accuracy by an average of 3% across six test sets compared with existing visual tool use work, by helping the model attend to the most relevant input evidence.

AI.tldr: IAPO (Input Attribution-Aware Policy Optimization) is an RL method that improves tool-calling in multimodal small language models by aligning model attributions with a teacher's, achieving 3% average accuracy gains on visual QA tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing RL methods for tool-calling in SLMs have limitations: 1) they rely on exact matching against ground-truth formats which is unsuitable for multimodal tasks where multiple valid tool paths exist, 2) sparse binary rewards provide poor guidance for learning decision processes in multimodal SLMs.
AI.method: Proposes IAPO (Input Attribution-Aware Policy Optimization), an RL algorithm that improves tool use in multimodal SLMs by aligning the model's attribution across input components with that of a stronger teacher model.
AI.result: Experiments on Qwen2.5-VL-3B show the method improves visual question answering accuracy by an average of 3% across six test sets compared to existing visual tool use approaches, by helping the model attend to relevant input evidence.
AI.conclusion: IAPO effectively addresses limitations of existing RL methods for multimodal SLM tool-calling by using attribution alignment with a teacher model, providing better learning signals than sparse binary rewards and enabling improved attention to relevant multimodal evidence.

title: Sparse probes and murky physics: a case study of interpretability challenges in a foundation model for continuum dynamics
summary: Generative AI emulators are increasingly used in scientific domains where we already have strong theory, benchmarks, and physical intuition. This raises a central evaluation and interpretability question: when a foundation-style model can reproduce known continuum dynamics, what internal mechanism supports that behavior, is the internal behaviour consistent with known physics, and how does it relate to where the emulator succeeds or fails? We investigate a cross-domain foundation model for continuum dynamics, Walrus by Polymathic, using mechanistic interpretability guided by physical principles. We apply a sparse autoencoder (SAE) to probe a selected layer, and address the practical challenge of triaging a large feature set (over 20,000) using enstrophy as a physically grounded metric. As a deliberately simple testbed, we focus on shear flow and compare feature recruitment across multiple shear-flow setups, i.e. parameter values in the numerical simulation. Across setups we find evidence of piecewise consistency, with subsets of features recurring in similar roles, but this structure is intermittent and does not map cleanly onto standard physical decompositions. In parallel, direct comparisons between numerical simulation and the emulator reveal systematic output-level discrepancies, including regimes where energy/structures become too diffuse or too localized. We connect parts of these discrepancies to changes in specific SAE feature usage. Our work highlights open questions for scientific foundation models: how to robustly prioritize mechanistically meaningful features, how to separate stable structure from analysis artifacts (including single-layer and SAE limitations), and how to use established benchmarks to decide when "different" internal representations are genuinely informative rather than merely effective.

AI.tldr: Researchers use mechanistic interpretability to analyze Walrus, a cross-domain foundation model for continuum dynamics, finding partial alignment with physics but intermittent feature consistency and systematic output discrepancies.
AI.motivation: To understand what internal mechanisms allow generative AI emulators to reproduce known continuum dynamics, whether these mechanisms align with known physics, and how they relate to the model's successes and failures - particularly for scientific foundation models where physical intuition already exists.
AI.method: Applied mechanistic interpretability guided by physical principles to Walrus model. Used sparse autoencoder (SAE) to probe selected layer, triaged over 20,000 features using enstrophy as physically grounded metric. Focused on shear flow testbed, comparing feature recruitment across multiple shear-flow setups with different parameter values.
AI.result: Found evidence of piecewise consistency with subsets of features recurring in similar roles, but structure is intermittent and doesn't map cleanly onto standard physical decompositions. Identified systematic output-level discrepancies between numerical simulation and emulator (energy/structures becoming too diffuse or localized). Connected some discrepancies to changes in specific SAE feature usage.
AI.conclusion: Highlights open questions for scientific foundation models: how to robustly prioritize mechanistically meaningful features, separate stable structure from analysis artifacts, and use established benchmarks to determine when "different" internal representations are genuinely informative versus merely effective.

title: Bergson: An Open Source Library for Data Attribution
summary: Data attribution is a promising field in interpretability that aims to explain model behavior through the influence of its training data, with applications including debugging undesirable model behavior and training dataset curation. However, significant engineering effort is required to perform it at scale, and many cutting edge techniques lack open-source tooling and support. Bergson is an open source library that aims to enable faster progress in the field by providing a host of techniques that scale to very large language models and pre-training datasets. The library natively supports on-disk gradient stores and multi-node distributed training, and provides quality of life tools for researchers. Finally, we introduce the first open-source implementations of three leading data attribution methods: MAGIC, SOURCE, and TrackStar. The library is available at https://github.com/EleutherAI/bergson .

AI.tldr: Bergson is an open-source library that provides scalable implementations of data attribution techniques for large language models, addressing the lack of tooling and engineering support in the field.
AI.motivation: Data attribution is important for interpreting model behavior and has applications in debugging and dataset curation, but there's significant engineering effort required to scale these techniques and a lack of open-source tooling for cutting-edge methods.
AI.method: Developed Bergson library with native support for on-disk gradient stores and multi-node distributed training, enabling scalability to very large language models and pre-training datasets. Includes quality of life tools for researchers.
AI.result: Created the first open-source implementations of three leading data attribution methods: MAGIC, SOURCE, and TrackStar. The library is available on GitHub for public use.
AI.conclusion: Bergson addresses the engineering challenges in data attribution research by providing scalable, open-source tooling that should accelerate progress in the field of interpretable AI.

title: Spectrally Regularized Latent Flow Matching for Turbulence Generation
comment: Accepted at the AI4Physics Workshop at ICML 2026. OpenReview: https://openreview.net/forum?id=MEZ1otYgXS
summary: Latent diffusion and flow matching have emerged as leading approaches for synthetic turbulence generation, yet they systematically under-represent dissipation-range amplitudes. We introduce a latent flow matching framework with a spectrally regularized compression stage that directly targets this failure mode. On a 256^2 DNS dataset at Re_f \approx 2250, replacing an MSE-trained VAE with a zone-weighted log-spectral objective raises deep-dissipation retained spectral power from 25% to 94% in reconstruction and from 20% to 79% in unconditional generation. The improved latent representation also yields a substantially better sampling cost-fidelity tradeoff: the MSE-trained latent space imposes a fundamental quality ceiling near DD bias -0.70 that no integrator or step-count can overcome, while the spectrally regularized latent space reaches DD bias -0.117 at just 20 function evaluations. Mechanistically, encoder-decoder swap experiments show that the improvement is driven primarily by encoder-induced latent reorganization rather than decoder capacity, while a support-amplitude decomposition reveals that MSE-trained models behave as conservative suppression models, minimizing pointwise error by attenuating intermittent high-wavenumber structure. Both pipelines recover the second-order structure function and the correct sign of S_3, indicating the correct cascade direction without explicit supervision. A small residual gap in the magnitude of S_3 suggests that phase-coherent triadic organization remains a complementary axis to amplitude fidelity for future generative turbulence models.

AI.tldr: Introduces spectrally regularized latent flow matching for turbulence generation, fixing systematic underestimation of dissipation-range amplitudes by replacing MSE-trained VAEs with zone-weighted log-spectral objectives.
AI.motivation: Existing latent diffusion and flow matching approaches for synthetic turbulence generation systematically under-represent dissipation-range amplitudes, creating a fundamental quality limitation in current methods.
AI.method: Proposes a latent flow matching framework with spectrally regularized compression stage using zone-weighted log-spectral objective instead of MSE-trained VAE. Tests on 256^2 DNS dataset at Re_f ≈ 2250, includes encoder-decoder swap experiments and support-amplitude decomposition analysis.
AI.result: Dramatically improves dissipation-range spectral power from 25% to 94% in reconstruction and 20% to 79% in generation. Eliminates fundamental quality ceiling, achieving DD bias -0.117 vs MSE's -0.70 limit. Better sampling cost-fidelity tradeoff (20 function evaluations vs unlimited MSE improvement).
AI.conclusion: Spectral regularization enables accurate dissipation-range turbulence generation by reorganizing latent space to preserve high-wavenumber structure. Both methods recover correct cascade direction, but residual S_3 magnitude gap indicates phase-coherent triadic organization remains a challenge for future models.

title: Noise-Aware Framework for Correcting Corrupted Labels
summary: High-quality labeled data is essential for training reliable ML/DL models. However, real-world datasets often contain a considerable proportion of corrupted labels, which can severely degrade model performance. To address this problem, we propose CANOLA, a novel framework for correcting corrupted labels through noise-aware learning and iterative label refinement. CANOLA explicitly estimates the underlying noise distribution of the dataset and incorporates this information into the training of a noise-aware Deep Neural Network. By incorporating noise characteristics during learning, CANOLA enables the model to down-weight unreliable supervision signals and focus on trustworthy patterns, thereby improving robustness and generalization. Label correction is performed via cautious, iterative soft label refinement, in which model predictions are blended with observed labels to prevent premature or erroneous updates. This progressive refinement allows the dataset to be repaired in a stable and controlled manner. We evaluate CANOLA on six widely used datasets under realistic noisy labeling scenarios. Experimental results show that CANOLA consistently outperforms SOTA label correction methods, achieving relative improvements ranging from 19% to 52% in error reduction. Moreover, models trained on datasets corrected by CANOLA obtain substantial downstream performance gains. Even simple classifiers trained on CANOLA's corrected data can outperform complex model-centric approaches by margins of up to 67%.

AI.tldr: CANOLA is a noise-aware learning framework that corrects corrupted labels by estimating dataset noise distribution and performing iterative soft label refinement, outperforming state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: Real-world datasets often contain corrupted labels that severely degrade ML/DL model performance, creating a need for robust label correction methods to improve training data quality.
AI.method: CANOLA estimates the underlying noise distribution of the dataset, trains a noise-aware Deep Neural Network that down-weights unreliable supervision, and performs cautious iterative soft label refinement by blending model predictions with observed labels.
AI.result: CANOLA outperforms SOTA label correction methods on six datasets with relative improvements of 19-52% in error reduction, and models trained on CANOLA-corrected data achieve substantial downstream performance gains, with simple classifiers outperforming complex model-centric approaches by up to 67%.
AI.conclusion: CANOLA provides an effective framework for correcting corrupted labels through noise-aware learning and iterative refinement, significantly improving data quality and downstream model performance across various noisy labeling scenarios.

title: A Data-Centric Framework for Detecting and Correcting Corrupted Labels
summary: The performance of machine learning and deep learning models largely depends on the quality of the training data. However, the quality of the real-world datasets is often compromised by noisy labels, which can substantially degrade model accuracy and reliability. To address this challenge, we propose Relabeler, an end-to-end data-centric framework for detecting and correcting corrupted labels. For corrupted label detection, Relabeler jointly leverages both local and global relationships among data instances to identify potentially noisy samples. After detecting suspicious instances, Relabeler further performs label correction by estimating the most probable clean label for each instance based on both its input features and observed noisy label. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets, noise types, and noise rates demonstrate that Relabeler consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving up to 58% improvement in label correction precision and 6% improvement in downstream task performance.

AI.tldr: Relabeler: An end-to-end data-centric framework that jointly leverages local and global data relationships to detect and correct noisy labels in datasets, improving both label correction precision and downstream task performance.
AI.motivation: Real-world datasets often contain noisy/corrupted labels that degrade machine learning and deep learning model performance, accuracy, and reliability. The quality of training data is crucial for model performance, but current methods don't adequately address the label noise problem.
AI.method: Relabeler is an end-to-end framework with two main components: 1) Corrupted label detection using both local and global relationships among data instances to identify potentially noisy samples, 2) Label correction that estimates the most probable clean label for each instance based on both input features and observed noisy label.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across multiple datasets, noise types, and noise rates show Relabeler consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving up to 58% improvement in label correction precision and 6% improvement in downstream task performance.
AI.conclusion: Relabeler effectively addresses the label noise problem by combining local and global data relationships for detection and correction, providing a robust solution for improving dataset quality and enhancing machine learning model performance across various real-world scenarios.

title: RLCSD: Reinforcement Learning with Contrastive On-Policy Self-Distillation
summary: On-policy self-distillation (OPSD) provides dense, token-level supervision for reasoning models by aligning a model's own distribution with the distribution it produces under privileged context, typically a verified solution. However, we show that the learning signal drawn from this distributional gap concentrates on style tokens rather than task-bearing ones, as the hinted model tends to produce more direct, shorter outputs. We term this pathology \emph{privilege-induced style drift}, which destabilizes training or causes response length to shrink. To address this, we propose \textbf{RLCSD} (Reinforcement Learning with Contrastive on-policy Self-Distillation), which mitigates this drift by contrasting the teacher-student gap under a correct hint against that under a wrong hint, suppressing the style shift that conditioning on a hint tends to induce regardless of correctness, and yielding a signal that is more concentrated on task-bearing tokens. Experiments on Qwen3 (1.7B/4B/8B) and Olmo-3-7B-Think across mathematical and logical reasoning show that RLCSD consistently outperforms GRPO and prior OPSD methods. We further show that the contrastive principle is general: it plugs into existing OPSD methods to improve them, and its underlying insight extends to the broader cross-model on-policy distillation setting.

AI.tldr: RLCSD (Reinforcement Learning with Contrastive on-policy Self-Distillation) addresses privilege-induced style drift in reasoning models by contrasting teacher-student gaps under correct vs. wrong hints, improving task-focused learning over previous OPSD methods.
AI.motivation: On-policy self-distillation (OPSD) suffers from privilege-induced style drift, where models learn to shorten outputs and focus on style tokens rather than task-bearing ones when conditioned on privileged context like verified solutions.
AI.method: Proposed RLCSD contrasts the teacher-student distribution gap under correct hints against wrong hints, suppressing style shifts induced by hint conditioning regardless of correctness, yielding more task-focused signals.
AI.result: RLCSD consistently outperforms GRPO and prior OPSD methods across Qwen3 (1.7B/4B/8B) and Olmo-3-7B-Think models on mathematical and logical reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: The contrastive principle is generalizable—it can plug into existing OPSD methods to improve them, and extends to broader cross-model on-policy distillation settings for better reasoning model training.

title: ICA Lens: Interpreting Language Models Without Training Another Dictionary
summary: Finding interpretable directions in language-model representations is critical for understanding and controlling model behavior. Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) have become the standard tool for this purpose, but using them as the default first lens often requires training, storing, and evaluating large overcomplete dictionaries. This bottleneck limits rapid exploration and raises a fundamental question: how much interpretable structure is already visible from activation geometry before training another neural dictionary? Our intuition is simple: many interpretable directions are selective on tokens, and these directions should look less Gaussian than random directions. We therefore revisit independent component analysis (ICA), a classical method for finding non-Gaussian directions, as a compact lens for language-model interpretability. We find that ICA has been underestimated for LLM interpretability, because prior uses often relied on off-the-shelf ICA implementations that are brittle on LLM activations and lacked systematic tools for inspecting and evaluating the recovered directions. To bridge these gaps, we introduce ICALens, the first practical workflow for stable, efficient, and auditable ICA analysis of LLM representations. It combines an optimized GPU-parallel FastICA pipeline with LLM-specific stability recipes and better fitting diagnostics, enabling efficient and reliable layer-wise analysis. Across GPT-2 Small, Gemma 2 2B, and Qwen 3.5 2B Base, ICALens efficiently recovers compact, human-interpretable directions without per-layer gradient-based dictionary training. On SAEBench, ICA is competitive with public SAEs in sparse probing and outperforms them in targeted probe perturbation under small-to-medium budgets. These results suggest that ICA should not be viewed as a weak baseline, but as an efficient and complementary first lens for exploring language-model representations.

AI.tldr: ICALens introduces a practical ICA workflow for LLM interpretability that efficiently finds interpretable directions without training large SAEs, showing competitive performance with lower computational cost.
AI.motivation: Current sparse autoencoder (SAE) approaches for finding interpretable directions in language models require training, storing, and evaluating large dictionaries, creating bottlenecks for rapid exploration. The authors question how much interpretable structure is already visible from activation geometry without additional neural dictionary training.
AI.method: They revisit independent component analysis (ICA), a classical method for finding non-Gaussian directions, creating ICALens - a practical workflow with optimized GPU-parallel FastICA, LLM-specific stability recipes, and better fitting diagnostics for stable and efficient layer-wise analysis of LLM representations.
AI.result: ICALens efficiently recovers compact, human-interpretable directions across GPT-2 Small, Gemma 2 2B, and Qwen 3.5 2B Base models without per-layer gradient-based dictionary training. On SAEBench, ICA is competitive with public SAEs in sparse probing and outperforms them in targeted probe perturbation under small-to-medium budgets.
AI.conclusion: ICA should not be viewed as a weak baseline but as an efficient and complementary first lens for exploring language-model representations, offering a more accessible alternative to SAEs for interpretability research.

title: Capacity-Constrained Online Convex Optimization with Delayed Feedback
summary: Online learning with delayed feedback typically assumes that the learner can track all pending rounds until their feedback arrives. In practice, tracking resources are finite, and feedback from untracked rounds is permanently lost. In this paper, we study delayed online convex optimization (OCO) under a hard capacity constraint, where at most $C$ pending rounds can be tracked at any time. To model delay information, we introduce a semi-clairvoyant model that refines the clairvoyant assumption from prior work: rather than requiring delays to be known at prediction time, the learner observes delay expirations online, consistent with the classical unconstrained delayed setting. Our approach proceeds via a reduction to a novel ``delayed and weighted'' OCO problem, using a scheduler that randomizes tracking decisions and importance-weights the resulting observations. For this base problem, we propose and analyze Delayed-Weighted FTRL and its bandit analogue, establishing regret bounds that explicitly characterize the interaction between time-varying weights and delayed feedback. Combining these base learners with our schedulers yields the first regret guarantees for capacity-constrained OCO under convex and strongly convex losses, for both first-order and bandit feedback. For first-order feedback, capacity $C = Ω(\log T)$ suffices to recover standard delayed OCO rates up to logarithmic factors. For bandit feedback, the regret rates are modulated by powers of $(1 + σ_{\text{max}}/C)$, where $σ_{\text{max}}$ is the maximum number of pending observations at any time. This allows the regret bound to degrade gracefully when $C < σ_{\text{max}}$, while remaining sublinear.

AI.tldr: This paper studies delayed online convex optimization with finite tracking capacity constraints, proposing a semi-clairvoyant model and reduction to a delayed weighted OCO problem with novel algorithms that provide sublinear regret bounds under both first-order and bandit feedback.
AI.motivation: Prior work on delayed online learning assumes infinite tracking capacity for pending feedback, but in practice, tracking resources are finite. When capacity is exceeded, feedback from untracked rounds is permanently lost, creating a realistic constraint not addressed in existing literature.
AI.method: 1) Introduce a semi-clairvoyant model that refines the clairvoyant assumption by observing delay expirations online. 2) Reduce the problem to a novel "delayed and weighted" OCO problem. 3) Use a scheduler that randomizes tracking decisions and importance-weights observations. 4) Propose Delayed-Weighted FTRL and its bandit variant for the base problem. 5) Combine base learners with schedulers to handle capacity constraints.
AI.result: First regret guarantees for capacity-constrained OCO under convex and strongly convex losses for both first-order and bandit feedback. For first-order feedback, capacity C = Ω(log T) suffices to recover standard delayed OCO rates up to logarithmic factors. For bandit feedback, regret rates depend on powers of (1 + σ_max/C), allowing graceful degradation when C < σ_max while maintaining sublinear regret.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully addresses the practical problem of finite tracking capacity in delayed online learning, providing theoretically sound algorithms with rigorous regret bounds that adapt to capacity constraints and recover standard results when sufficient capacity is available.

title: AI4Land: Scalable Deep Learning for Global High-Resolution Land Use Reconstruction
summary: Uncertainty in the terrestrial carbon cycle remains a major constraint in climate projections, partly driven by the uncertainties affecting the land surface representation and variability in Earth system models. To address this limitation, we present a data-driven framework AI4Land, for generating high-resolution historical reconstructions and future projections of key land surface variables. The framework follows a two-phase approach using a U-Net architecture. In the first phase, which is the focus of this work, it reconstructs annual land use and land cover by integrating coarse-resolution scenario data with static geophysical features. In a planned second phase, the resulting high-resolution maps will be used to predict dynamic biophysical variables, particularly leaf area index, at finer temporal scales. Trained on Earth observation data, the models learn to reproduce spatially explicit and physically consistent land surface patterns, extending temporal coverage to periods lacking direct observations. AI4Land was developed and trained on MareNostrum5, demonstrating how GPU-accelerated HPC infrastructure enables global-scale climate AI pipelines. The final product is a suite of open-source emulators designed for real-time coupling with digital twin platforms, such as those developed under the Destination Earth initiative. By delivering realistic and evolving land surface conditions on demand, this work aims to reduce critical uncertainties and improve the predictive power of next-generation climate simulations.

AI.tldr: AI4Land is a data-driven U-Net framework that generates high-resolution land surface reconstructions and projections to reduce uncertainties in climate models, trained on Earth observation data for coupling with digital twin platforms.
AI.motivation: Uncertainty in the terrestrial carbon cycle remains a major constraint in climate projections, partly due to uncertainties in land surface representation and variability in Earth system models. This limits climate prediction accuracy.
AI.method: Two-phase approach using U-Net architecture: Phase 1 reconstructs annual land use and land cover by integrating coarse-resolution scenario data with static geophysical features. Phase 2 will use these maps to predict dynamic biophysical variables like leaf area index at finer temporal scales. Trained on Earth observation data using GPU-accelerated HPC infrastructure (MareNostrum5).
AI.result: Developed AI4Land framework that learns to reproduce spatially explicit and physically consistent land surface patterns, extending temporal coverage to periods lacking direct observations. Created open-source emulators for real-time coupling with digital twin platforms like Destination Earth initiative.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables realistic, evolving land surface conditions on demand, aiming to reduce critical uncertainties and improve predictive power of next-generation climate simulations for better climate projections.

title: Multimodal Ordinal Modeling of Alzheimer's Disease Severity Using Structural MRI and Clinical Data
comment: 18 pages. Submitted to journal for review
summary: Neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's disease (AD) require accurate and scalable tools for assessing disease severity, yet current clinical staging remains time-intensive and prone to variability. We propose an attention-enhanced multimodal machine learning framework with ordinal regression for automated and interpretable AD severity staging. The framework integrates T1-weighted MRI with demographic and genetic variables and compares unimodal and multimodal architectures using ordinal and non-ordinal prediction heads. Models were trained and validated using cohort-stratified splits derived from the ADNI, AIBL, and NIFD datasets. A strictly held-out test set was constructed using subjects excluded from all training, validation, preprocessing, and hyperparameter tuning procedures, with subject-level splitting employed throughout to prevent data leakage. Among unimodal approaches, the T1-weighted MRI model achieved slightly higher adjacent-stage accuracy (0.963) and agreement with clinical staging (QWK 0.444) than the tabular model (QWK 0.433). Integrating imaging, demographic, and genetic information improved overall performance. The multimodal non-ordinal baseline achieved the lowest prediction error (MAE 0.340), whereas the ordinal multimodal model achieved the highest adjacent-stage accuracy (0.970) and strongest agreement with clinical staging (QWK 0.549). These findings indicate that ordinal formulations better capture the ordered structure of the CDR scale and yield predictions more consistent with clinical staging. Explainability analyses using Grad CAM++ and SHAP demonstrated anatomically and clinically plausible model behavior, supporting transparent decision-making. Overall, attention-based multimodal learning with ordinal regression represents a robust, interpretable, and scalable approach for automated AD severity staging and AI-assisted clinical decision support.

AI.tldr: Multimodal AI framework combining brain MRI with demographic/genetic data and ordinal regression improves automated Alzheimer's disease severity staging with better clinical agreement and interpretability.
AI.motivation: Current clinical staging of Alzheimer's disease is time-intensive and prone to variability, requiring more accurate and scalable assessment tools.
AI.method: Attention-enhanced multimodal machine learning framework with ordinal regression that integrates T1-weighted MRI with demographic and genetic variables, comparing unimodal vs. multimodal architectures using ordinal and non-ordinal prediction heads.
AI.result: Multimodal models outperformed unimodal approaches - ordinal multimodal model achieved highest adjacent-stage accuracy (0.970) and strongest agreement with clinical staging (QWK 0.549), while non-ordinal baseline had lowest prediction error (MAE 0.340). Explainability analyses (Grad CAM++, SHAP) showed anatomically and clinically plausible model behavior.
AI.conclusion: Ordinal formulations better capture the ordered structure of clinical dementia rating scales, and attention-based multimodal learning with ordinal regression represents a robust, interpretable, and scalable approach for automated AD severity staging and AI-assisted clinical decision support.

title: RCAP: Robust, Class-Aware, Probabilistic Dynamic Dataset Pruning
comment: Proceedings of the Forty-first Conference on Uncertainty in Artificial Intelligence (UAI 2025)
summary: Dynamic data pruning techniques aim to reduce computational cost while minimizing information loss by periodically selecting representative subsets of input data during model training. However, existing methods often struggle to maintain strong worst-group accuracy, particularly at high pruning rates, across balanced and imbalanced datasets. To address this challenge, we propose RCAP, a Robust, Class-Aware, Probabilistic dynamic dataset pruning algorithm for classification tasks. RCAP applies a closed-form solution to estimate the fraction of samples to be included in the training subset for each individual class. This fraction is adaptively adjusted in every epoch using class-wise aggregated loss. Thereafter, it employs an adaptive sampling strategy that prioritizes samples having high loss for populating the class-wise subsets. We evaluate RCAP on six diverse datasets ranging from class-balanced to highly imbalanced using five distinct models across three training paradigms: training from scratch, transfer learning, and fine-tuning. Our approach consistently outperforms state-of-the-art dataset pruning methods, achieving superior worst-group accuracy at all pruning rates. Remarkably, with only $10\%$ data, RCAP delivers $>1\%$ improvement in performance on class-imbalanced datasets compared to full data training while providing an average $8.69\times$ speedup. The code can be accessed at https://github.com/atif-hassan/RCAP-dynamic-dataset-pruning

AI.tldr: RCAP is a dynamic dataset pruning method that uses class-aware probabilistic sampling to maintain strong worst-group accuracy, especially for imbalanced datasets, achieving better performance with only 10% data than full-data training in some cases.
AI.motivation: Existing dynamic data pruning methods struggle to maintain worst-group accuracy at high pruning rates, particularly across both balanced and imbalanced datasets. There's a need for methods that can reduce computational costs while preserving performance for all groups, especially minority classes in imbalanced scenarios.
AI.method: RCAP uses a class-aware probabilistic approach with: 1) A closed-form solution to estimate the fraction of samples to include per class, adaptively adjusted each epoch using class-wise aggregated loss; 2) An adaptive sampling strategy that prioritizes high-loss samples for populating class-wise subsets.
AI.result: RCAP outperforms state-of-the-art dataset pruning methods across six diverse datasets (balanced to highly imbalanced) using five models and three training paradigms. With only 10% data, it achieves >1% improvement on imbalanced datasets compared to full data training, with 8.69× average speedup, while maintaining superior worst-group accuracy at all pruning rates.
AI.conclusion: RCAP effectively addresses the challenge of maintaining worst-group accuracy in dynamic dataset pruning, particularly for imbalanced datasets, demonstrating that significant computational savings can be achieved without sacrificing (and sometimes improving) performance compared to full-data training.

title: From Uniform to Learned Graph Priors: Diffusion for Structure Discovery
summary: Neural relational inference (NRI) methods discover interaction graphs from trajectories through variational reasoning on discrete potential edges. However, these methods typically rely on oversimplified, factorized graph priors. Such priors, typically nearing uniform distributions, treat edges as independent entities. This systemic misalignment does not match the real-world systems and yields diffuse and indecisive edge posteriors limiting the reliability of structural discovery. To address this, we propose \textit{Diff-prior}, a diffusion-parameterized adaptive prior used to calibrate latent graph distribution rather than generate graphs. Our core insight is to reframe prior integration as a learnable denoising-style calibration that organizes scattered, uncertain edge posteriors into a more reliable overall structure which can be trained by the diffusion model. Diff-prior learns an adaptive structure prior that performs structured calibration on the edge posteriors during inference, guiding it towards a distribution closer to the underlying structure. The diff-prior operates before structural sampling and acts as a denoising calibrator directly on the encoder edge distribution, which provides a generic training paradigm over structured variables. Experiments on standard benchmarks validated our framework, and the results indicate that Diff-prior improves the performance of structure inference and generates more decisive edge posteriors across multiple NRI-family architectures. The code is available on https://github.com/Hardy158118/Diffprior.

AI.tldr: Diff-prior improves neural relational inference by using a diffusion-based adaptive prior to calibrate latent graph distributions, producing more reliable and decisive edge posteriors for structure discovery.
AI.motivation: Current NRI methods rely on oversimplified, factorized graph priors (near uniform distributions) that treat edges independently. This misalignment with real-world systems produces diffuse and indecisive edge posteriors, limiting reliable structural discovery.
AI.method: Diff-prior: a diffusion-parameterized adaptive prior that reframes prior integration as learnable denoising-style calibration. It organizes scattered, uncertain edge posteriors into more reliable overall structures by performing structured calibration on edge posteriors during inference before structural sampling.
AI.result: Experiments on standard benchmarks show Diff-prior improves structure inference performance and generates more decisive edge posteriors across multiple NRI-family architectures.
AI.conclusion: Diff-prior provides a generic training paradigm for structured variables that calibrates latent graph distributions to be closer to underlying structures, enhancing the reliability of neural relational inference methods.

title: Fine-tuning Multi-modal LLMs with ART: Art-based Reinforcement Training
summary: There are two main Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) techniques for Large Language Models (LLMs). While Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) introduces additional weights between the LLM layers, Soft Prompting introduces additional fine-tuning-specific raw tokens to an LLM input. However, both require modification to the computational graphs of precompiled, preoptimized LLMs. As a result, neither is fully supported in high-throughput engines like vLLM. We propose fine-tuning with ART (Art-based Reinforcement Training). The method injects information into a frozen Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) by optimizing only its raw visual input, thus enabling the soft-token approach on pre-compiled computational graphs. It relies on backpropagation of gradients back into a plain pixel array and thus supports any fine-tuning objective. Moreover, the optimized visual input can be stylized as task-relevant computational artworks. The approach's effectiveness is confirmed for different sizes of a popular open Qwen architecture and for several textual benchmarks. Specifically, ART reaches accuracy competitive with LoRA across mathematics and structured-tool-use benchmarks.

AI.tldr: ART (Art-based Reinforcement Training) is a novel Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning method for Multimodal LLMs that optimizes only the raw visual input pixels while keeping the model frozen, enabling soft-token fine-tuning on precompiled models.
AI.motivation: Existing PEFT techniques like LoRA and Soft Prompting require modifications to computational graphs of precompiled, preoptimized LLMs, which prevents their use in high-throughput engines like vLLM. There's a need for fine-tuning methods that work with frozen models without altering their computational structure.
AI.method: ART injects information into a frozen MLLM by optimizing only its raw visual input pixels through backpropagation. The method works by backpropagating gradients into a plain pixel array, enabling any fine-tuning objective. The optimized visual inputs can be stylized as task-relevant computational artworks.
AI.result: ART achieves accuracy competitive with LoRA across mathematics and structured-tool-use benchmarks. The approach was validated on different sizes of the Qwen architecture and several textual benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: ART provides a practical alternative to existing PEFT methods that enables fine-tuning on precompiled LLMs without computational graph modifications, while also creating interpretable, artwork-style visual inputs as a byproduct of the optimization process.

title: Beyond representational alignment with brain-guided language models for robust reasoning
summary: The correspondence between large language models (LLMs) and the neural mechanisms underlying human higher-order cognition remains insufficiently characterized. Given that language and reasoning in the human brain appear dissociable, an open question is whether LLMs align with neural signals from reasoning-related regions and whether such signals can improve them. Here, focusing on deductive reasoning, we show that LLM internal representations are not only partially aligned with task-fMRI activity but can also be directly enhanced by these signals. Using a neural-predictivity metric, we find that LLMs explain a substantial fraction of the explainable variance in reasoning-related regions at the aggregate level, whereas predictivity within specific reasoning types is lower, indicating both alignment and divergence. Building on this, we propose a brain-guided framework: we steer model representations along directions induced by the joint structure of model and brain representations, applying intervention at inference and fine-tuning during training. We demonstrate that task-evoked brain signals can directly enhance LLM reasoning, yielding gains orthogonal to language-only supervision across 10 LLMs (1.5B-72B), with transfer across reasoning types and up to 13\% absolute accuracy gain. Our results advance LLM-brain correspondences from correlation to guidance, establishing a brain-signal-driven pathway toward more robust and cognitively aligned AI.

AI.tldr: Brain signals from human reasoning regions can directly enhance LLM performance on deductive reasoning tasks, with up to 13% accuracy gains across various model sizes.
AI.motivation: The alignment between LLMs and neural mechanisms of human higher-order cognition remains poorly understood, particularly since language and reasoning appear dissociable in the brain. There's an open question about whether LLMs align with reasoning-related neural signals and whether such signals could actually improve LLM performance.
AI.method: Researchers used task-fMRI to study deductive reasoning, developed a neural-predictivity metric to measure alignment between LLM representations and brain activity, and created a brain-guided framework that steers LLM representations along directions induced by joint structure of model and brain representations through inference interventions and fine-tuning during training.
AI.result: LLMs explain substantial explainable variance in reasoning-related brain regions at aggregate level (showing partial alignment), though predictivity within specific reasoning types is lower (showing divergence). Brain-guided framework yielded orthogonal gains to language-only supervision across 10 LLMs (1.5B-72B parameters), with transfer across reasoning types and up to 13% absolute accuracy improvement.
AI.conclusion: This work advances LLM-brain correspondences from mere correlation to active guidance, establishing a brain-signal-driven pathway toward more robust and cognitively aligned AI systems that leverage human reasoning mechanisms to enhance machine performance.

title: Space-sampled Value Decay: Forgetting Mechanisms for Non-stationary Deep Reinforcement Learning
comment: Accepted at The 2nd Workshop on Epistemic Intelligence in Machine Learning, EIML@ICML 2026, (non-archival)
summary: Studies on rodents such as mice have shown the capabilities to adapt their behavior when dealing with changing parameters (``drift'') of the environment even if no information about change is provided (uncertainty) -- a behavior that can be modeled by forgetting mechanisms. Non-stationary Reinforcement Learning (NSRL) deals with adapting state-of-the-art RL methods to deal with changing environments: these however usually require (partially) perfect information about the drift such as ``task IDs'' or ``context''. To mitigate the effects of drift, this work develops \emph{Space-sampled Value Decay} as an explicit forgetting mechanism for value-based deep RL architectures as a simple yet effective approach. In particular we demonstrate and discuss positive effects but also limitations in achieved returns for modifications of Deep Q-networks (DQN) and Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) when evaluated on non-stationary environments.

AI.tldr: Space-sampled Value Decay is proposed as an explicit forgetting mechanism for value-based deep RL to handle non-stationary environments without needing perfect information about environmental changes.
AI.motivation: Current non-stationary RL methods typically require perfect or partial information about environmental changes (like task IDs or context), but this work aims to develop methods that can handle drift without such information, inspired by forgetting mechanisms observed in rodent behavior studies.
